query: For vacuum induction furnace annealing of steels and alloys, summarize best practices for controlling grain growth while achieving stress relief/homogenization. Include practical soak temperature-time guidance ranges and cite high-quality sources with bibtex.

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor
query.title: Query

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: For vacuum induction furnace annealing of steels and alloys, summarize best practices for controlling grain growth while achieving stress relief/homogenization. Include practical soak temperature-time guidance ranges and cite high-quality sources with bibtex.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

function.name: create_plan

Plan registered and formatted correctly, with some modifications:
{
  "objectives": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "objective": "Search for literature on vacuum induction furnace annealing of steels and alloys, focusing on grain growth control during stress relief and homogenization",
      "rationale": "Need to find high-quality sources covering vacuum annealing practices, temperature-time parameters, and grain growth control strategies",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null

    {
      "id": 2,
      "objective": "Search for practical guidance on soak temperature and time ranges for vacuum annealing of steels and alloys",

    {
      "id": 3,

      "rationale": "Understanding grain growth mechanisms is essential for providing best practices",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null

Updated plan:

## 1. Design of vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on GA-Fuzzy-PID algorithm
Valid Text Names:
'meng2023designofvacuum pages 1-2', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 2-4', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 4-7', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 7-8', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 8-11', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 11-14', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 14-17', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 17-20', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 20-23', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 23-24', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 24-25'

BibTex:
@article{meng2023designofvacuum,
    author = "Meng, Jintao and Gao, Haitao and Ruan, Mixue and Guo, Hai and Zhou, Xiaojie and Zhang, Di",
    title = "Design of vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on GA-Fuzzy-PID algorithm",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "PLOS ONE",
    volume = "18",
    pages = "e0293823",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.1371/journal.pone.0293823",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0293823",
    publisher = "Public Library of Science (PLoS)",
    issue = "11",
    issn = "1932-6203"
}


Abstract:
As is well known, the metal annealing process has the characteristics of heat concentration and rapid heating. Traditional vacuum annealing furnaces use PID control method, which has problems such as high temperature fluctuation, large overshoot, and long response time during the heating and heating process. Based on this situation, some domestic scholars have adopted fuzzy PID control algorithm in the temperature control of vacuum annealing furnaces. Due to the fact that fuzzy rules are formulated through a large amount of on-site temperature data and experience summary, there is a certain degree of subjectivity, which cannot ensure that each rule is optimal. In response to this drawback, the author combined the technical parameters of vacuum annealing furnace equipment, The fuzzy PID temperature control of the vacuum annealing furnace is optimized using genetic algorithm. Through simulation and comparative analysis, it is concluded that the design of the fuzzy PID vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on GA optimization is superior to fuzzy PID and traditional PID control in terms of temperature accuracy, rise time, and overshoot control. Finally, it was verified through offline experiments that the fuzzy PID temperature control system based on GA optimization meets the annealing temperature requirements of metal workpieces and can be applied to the temperature control system of vacuum annealing furnaces.

Abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Downstream processing of steel products involves a large variety of heat treatments in the ferrite as well as in the austenite phase. Different from austenite conditioning in the upstream processing stage, deformation and temperature schedules are usually decoupled in downstream processing. Fundamental characteristics of microalloying elements regarding their effects on recovery, recrystallization, phase transformation, and grain growth during downstream processing will be reviewed. Relevant characteristics of microalloying elements are the atom size, the solubility and temperature stability of related carbides or nitrides, the tendency of segregating at grain boundaries, dislocations and point defects, as well as the diffusivity. The various interactions and resulting microstructural effects will be demonstrated for several industrial steel developments. The selected examples cover microalloy effects in ferrite and austenite phases, under slow and fast thermal cycles, as well as during short and long heat treatment times. Based on the observations and the related interpretation, a qualitative understanding of the microalloy interactions will be developed that is generally applicable despite the considerable variation of processes and products. It will also be highlighted how appropriate process and microalloy adaptations resulted in remarkable product optimization under industrial conditions.</jats:p>

## 3. Study of Grain Growth in a Ni-Based Superalloy by Experiments and Cellular Automaton Model
Valid Text Names:
'liu2021studyofgrain pages 1-2', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 2-5', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 5-6', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 6-8', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 8-10', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 10-11'

BibTex:
@article{liu2021studyofgrain,
    author = "Liu, Yan-Xing and Ke, Zhi-Jiang and Li, Run-Hua and Song, Ju-Qing and Ruan, Jing-Jing",
    title = "Study of Grain Growth in a Ni-Based Superalloy by Experiments and Cellular Automaton Model",
    year = "2021",
    journal = "Materials",
    volume = "14",
    pages = "6922",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.3390/ma14226922",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/ma14226922",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "22",
    issn = "1996-1944"
}


Abstract:
The grain growth behavior in a typical Ni-based superalloy was investigated using isothermal heat treatment experiments over a holding temperature range of 1353–1473 K. The experimental results showed that the grain structure continuously coarsened as the holding time and holding temperature increased during heat treatment. A classical parabolic grain growth model was used to explore the mechanism of grain growth under experimental conditions. The grain growth exponent was found to be slightly above 2. This indicates that the current grain growth in the studied superalloy is mainly governed by grain boundary migration with a minor pinning effect from the precipitates. Then, the grain growth in the studied superalloy during isothermal heat treatment was modelled by a cellular automaton (CA) with deterministic state switch rules. The microscale kinetics of grain growth is described by the correlation between the moving velocity and curvature of the grain boundary. The local grain boundary curvature is well evaluated by a template disk method. The grain boundary mobility was found to increase with increasing temperature. The relationship between the grain boundary mobility and temperature has been established. The developed CA model is capable of capturing the dependence of the grain size on the holding time under different holding temperatures.

Abstract:
<jats:p>The wear behaviour of AISI 431stainless steel was investigated at consistent load under a dry sliding touch with vacuum annealing device. The approach implemented was found to have been effective in improving the wear actions of stainless steel products, advancing the pliability properties. For Vacuum Annealed (VA1) 2 hours, Vacuum Annealed (VA2) 4 hours and Vacuum Annealed (VA3) 6 hours respectively, a low temperature heat treatment was performed on sample for the maximum annealing cycle. Untreated stainless steel (UNT) was used as a kind of viewpoint tool for contrast with specific hardening samples and conducted wear checks. The hardening samples show certain hard formed layers which enhance the hardness of the surface. Estimations of small scale toughness showed a crucial improvement in toughness following operation. This method raises the hardness of the substance up to 150 percent. A pin on the disc drive is used to do wear checking to assess wear failure. The layers were represented by optical lens magnification and the examination of electron magnifying glass.</jats:p>

## 2. Kinetic Analysis of the Austenite Grain Growth in GCr15 Steel
Valid Text Names:
'yue2010kineticanalysisof pages 1-2', 'yue2010kineticanalysisof pages 2-4', 'yue2010kineticanalysisof pages 4-4'

    author = "Yue, Chongxiang and Zhang, Liwen and Liao, Shulun and Gao, Huiju",
    title = "Kinetic Analysis of the Austenite Grain Growth in GCr15 Steel",
    year = "2010",
    journal = "Journal of Materials Engineering and Performance",
    volume = "19",
    pages = "112-115",
    month = "Feb",
    doi = "10.1007/s11665-009-9413-y",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1007/s11665-009-9413-y",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "1059-9495"
}


Abstract:
The growth behavior of austenite grains in GCr15 steel was investigated through the isothermal annealing tests of the steel under different heating temperatures and holding times. The tests were performed on a Gleeble-3800 thermo-mechanical simulation machine. Austenitizing temperatures 1223, 1323, 1373, and 1423 K were chosen, and holding time varied from 0 to 480 s. Experimental results suggest that austenite grains grow gradually with the increase of heating temperature, and holding time has an important effect on the growth of austenite grains. The time exponent for the growth is bigger at higher temperature, and the growth rate decreases with increasing time. On the basis of previous models and experimental results, a mathematical model that can describe the growth behavior of austenite grains in the tested steel under different heating temperatures and holding times was obtained using regression analysis. The predicted grain sizes by the model are in good agreement with measured ones.

## 6. Microstructural evolution, recovery and recrystallization kinetics of isothermally annealed ultra low carbon steel
Valid Text Names:
'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 1-4', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 4-6', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 6-11', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 11-14', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 14-17', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 17-20', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 20-22', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 22-25'

BibTex:
@article{dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery,
    author = "Dutta, Siuli and Panda, Ashis K and Mitra, Amitava and Chatterjee, Subrata and Roy, Rajat K",
    title = "Microstructural evolution, recovery and recrystallization kinetics of isothermally annealed ultra low carbon steel",
    year = "2020",
    journal = "Materials Research Express",
    volume = "7",
    pages = "016554",
    month = "Jan",
    doi = "10.1088/2053-1591/ab657c",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1088/2053-1591/ab657c",
    publisher = "IOP Publishing",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "2053-1591"
}


Abstract:
The recovery and recrystallization kinetics of 80% cold rolled ultra low carbon steel are investigated during isothermally annealing for temperature ranges 350–640 °C as a function of different annealing time. The recovery is assessed by magnetic coercivity (Hc), while the recrystallization is determined by mechanical hardness. At low temperature (350 to 520 °C) annealing, recovery dominates for long time (∼12 000 s), while the annealing at 550 °C/ 900s and 580 °C/ 300s causes the recrystallized nuclei formation . The recovery kinetics is introduced by differential rate equation, explaining the reduction in coercivity with the recovery progress and the variation of an activation energy from 41–113 kJ mol−1. The recrystallization kinetics is found faster at high annealing temperature 640 °C than 550 and 580 °C based on hardness measurement, justifying by apparent activation energy within 114–190 kJ mol−1. Furthermore, the recovery and recrystallization rate increase with different annealing time, consistent to the change of microstructures and grain boundary characteristics evaluated by the orientation imaging microscopy (OIM) of electron backscattered diffraction (EBSD).

function.name: paper_search

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'hirota2008recrystallizationandgrain pages 6-7'.

Noriaki Hirota, Fuxing Yin, Tadanobu Inoue, and Tsukasa Azuma. Recrystallization and grain growth behavior in severe cold-rolling deformed sus316l steel under anisothermal annealing condition. Isij International, 48:475-482, Apr 2008. URL: https://doi.org/10.2355/isijinternational.48.475, doi:10.2355/isijinternational.48.475. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports anisothermal annealing experiments on cold-rolled SUS316L between 1 073 and 1 223 K with heating rates 0.031–9.3 K/s. It shows that the critical recrystallization finish temperature (Ti) increases approximately linearly with heating rate and that heating rate effects on grain growth are stronger when modeled using an empirical exp(0.5/q) factor rather than 1/q. High heating rates produce many dispersed recrystallization nucleation sites and yield an ideal lognormal grain-size distribution and ultrafine annealed microstructures; low heating rates allow longer dwell at elevated temperature, nucleation at preferred deformation features, and selective grain growth of some recrystallized grains. The kinetic model used is valid only for a strictly linear heating profile, and a more accurate treatment is needed when heating-rate nonlinearity exists. Controlling grain size therefore requires (a) choice of annealing temperature and heating rate (higher rates favor finer grains and more nucleation), (b) control of time-at-temperature (slow heating increases dwell time and grain growth), and (c) a homogeneous deformation microstructure (e.g., multipass rolling or grain-boundary engineering) to promote simultaneous nucleation and ultrafine final grain size. The paper also fits grain-growth parameters (K, n) and notes they correlate with isothermal values when appropriately adjusted for heating rate.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'digges1966heattreatmentand pages 13-15'.

Thomas G Digges and Samuel J Rosenberg. Heat treatment and properties of iron and steel. ArXiv, Oct 1966. URL: https://doi.org/10.6028/nbs.mono.18, doi:10.6028/nbs.mono.18. This article has 94 citations.

The excerpt gives general annealing guidance relevant to vacuum annealing for stress relief and homogenization though it does not discuss vacuum specifically. For full annealing, steels are heated above the transformation range (Acs) and typically held at about 25–50°F above the upper-critical line. Process (stress-relief) annealing is performed close to but below Aci; if no further cold work is intended, a lower relief range of about 1,000°F is often sufficient. Examples in the text show process annealing at ~1,200°F and full annealing at ~1,650°F. A commonly cited soak-time rule is "% hr/in. of thickness" (soak time scales with section thickness). The goal of soaking is to secure uniform temperature throughout the part; thicker sections therefore govern required soak time. Spheroidizing is achieved by prolonged heating just below Aci (final hold just below Ari), typically near ~1,000°F for high-carbon steels. Grain growth is influenced by temperature and time (full annealing produces larger ferrite grains than process annealing), so control is achieved by limiting soak temperature/time, using lower-temperature stress-relief cycles where possible, ensuring uniform heating (preheating/transfers to reduce local overheating), and matching soak to section thickness.


---

## pqac-00000002

From text 'hirota2008recrystallizationandgrain pages 1-2'.

Noriaki Hirota, Fuxing Yin, Tadanobu Inoue, and Tsukasa Azuma. Recrystallization and grain growth behavior in severe cold-rolling deformed sus316l steel under anisothermal annealing condition. Isij International, 48:475-482, Apr 2008. URL: https://doi.org/10.2355/isijinternational.48.475, doi:10.2355/isijinternational.48.475. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This study on SUS316L austenitic stainless steel examines anisothermal annealing (1073–1223 K) with heating rates from 0.031 to 9.3 K/s and links heating rate and temperature to recrystallization and grain growth. Recrystallization finish temperature was identified by a hardness criterion and found to vary linearly with the logarithm of heating rate. At the defined critical temperature the average grain size was ≈2.2 mm at low heating rates, but grain size decreased rapidly for heating rates above ~1 K/s. A grain-growth kinetics expression for anisothermal annealing was applied; the proportional term P was found to follow P = K·exp(0.5/q), and the grain growth exponent n was determined between 2.5 and 3.0. EBSD showed that low heating rates produced partial recrystallization with preferred orientations at completion, whereas high heating rates (>1 K/s) produced homogeneously nucleated, randomly oriented grains with a lognormal size distribution. The paper therefore indicates that fast heating (higher q) and control of peak temperature/time strongly influence final grain size and microstructure during annealing.

## 1. Precipitation and grain growth modelling in Ti-Nb microalloyed steels

@article{graux2019precipitationandgrain,
    author = "Graux, Alexis and Cazottes, Sophie and Castro, David De and Martín, David San and Capdevila, Carlos and Cabrera, Jose Maria and Molas, Sílvia and Schreiber, Sebastian and Mirković, Djordje and Danoix, Frédéric and Bugnet, Matthieu and Fabrègue, Damien and Perez, Michel",
    title = "Precipitation and grain growth modelling in Ti-Nb microalloyed steels",
    year = "2019",
    journal = "Materialia",
    volume = "5",
    pages = "100233",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.1016/j.mtla.2019.100233",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtla.2019.100233",
    publisher = "Elsevier BV",
    issn = "2589-1529"
}


Abstract:
Abstract Mechanical properties of microalloyed steels are enhanced by fine precipitates, that ensure grain growth control during subsequent heat treatment. This study aims at predicting austenite grain growth kinetics coupling a precipitation model and a grain growth model. These models were applied to a titanium and niobium microalloyed steel. The precipitate size distributions were first characterized by TEM and SEM and prior austenite grain boundaries were revealed by thermal etching after various isothermal treatments. From CALPHAD database, a solubility product was determined for (Ti,Nb)C precipitates. A numerical model based on the classical nucleation and growth theories was used to predict the time evolution of (Ti,Nb)C size distributions during various isothermal heat treatments. The precipitation model was validated from TEM/SEM analysis. The resulting precipitate size distributions served as entry parameters to a simple grain growth model based on Zener pinning. The pinning pressure was calculated using the whole size distribution. The resulting austenite grain growth kinetics were in good agreement with the experimental data obtained for all investigated heat treatments.

Abstract:
The steel industry is interested in developing high strength steels for lightweight applications. Typically, austenitic stainless steels have good ductility and formability but their yield strength is quite low.1) There are several methods to improve the strength, e.g., solid solute strengthening, work hardening and grain size refinement.2) Many austenitic stainless steels are metastable at the room temperature so that during cold working some strain-induced martensite (SIM) forms, thereby increasing their strength3) but reducing ductility.2) Therefore, other strengthening methods such as grain size refinement are preferred.4–8) An ultrafine grain size can be obtained by heavy cold deformation to create a high fraction of SIM that during subsequent proper annealing treatment reverts back to austenite. Martensitic reversion has already been investigated and reported during the 1970’s, as referred by Tomimura et al.4) They also noted the formation of a fine austenite grain size obtained from reversed martensite in their experimental Cr–Ni steels. Since then, grain refinement by the martensitic reversion treatment in both Cr–Ni and Cr–Mn austenitic stainless steels has been considered in numerous papers, Effect of Nb Microalloying on Reversion and Grain Growth in a High-Mn 204Cu Austenitic Stainless Steel

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000003

From text 'cheruvathur2016additivemanufacturingof pages 7-11'.

Sudha Cheruvathur, Eric A. Lass, and Carelyn E. Campbell. Additive manufacturing of 17-4 ph stainless steel: post-processing heat treatment to achieve uniform reproducible microstructure. JOM, 68:930-942, Dec 2016. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11837-015-1754-4, doi:10.1007/s11837-015-1754-4. This article has 378 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt documents heat-treatment behavior of additively manufactured 17-4 PH stainless steel relevant to stress relief and homogenization. For cast/alloy analogs, AMS 5355 recommends a homogenization heat treatment at 1150 C for >90 min followed by solutionizing at 1050 C to reduce segregation and approach a more uniform microstructure. The MC carbide solvus lies above ~1250 C, so treatments below this (including 1050 C solutionizing) will not fully dissolve MC carbides, explaining observed carbide coarsening after solutionizing. At 1050 C the equilibrium is FCC-Austenite + MC niobium carbides. Stress-relieving (EOS) treatments increased hardness (reported 312 ± 17 VHN) while still retaining some FCC/austenite; more aggressive anneals (AM-S) produced predominantly martensite and reduced retained austenite to ~15–20% but niobium carbides still coarsened, indicating anneal times/temperatures were insufficient to fully homogenize microchemistry. The text also notes microsegregation is present and that multiple tempering cycles cause "coarsening of carbides," while cryogenic treatments (dry ice) can modify secondary carbide populations. These points indicate temperature thresholds and limits for carbide dissolution and homogenization, and highlight trade-offs between hardness, retained phases, and carbide coarsening.


---

## pqac-00000004

From text 'tian2009experimentalandsimulation pages 6-7'.

Gaofeng Tian, Chengchang Jia, Jiantao Liu, and Benfu Hu. Experimental and simulation on the grain growth of p/m nickel-base superalloy during the heat treatment process. Materials & Design, 30:433-439, Mar 2009. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007, doi:10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007. This article has 71 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports a simulation and experimental study of gradient heat treatment of a P/M nickel-base superalloy disk. The authors used a furnace temperature 20–40°C above the c0 solvus (1150°C in this study). Temperature was monitored at six radial points; with increasing hold time the radial temperature gradient reduced and temperatures tended toward homogeneity. At a 60 min hold the inner points A–C were below 1110°C while outer points D–F were 1110–1145°C, implying inner regions grow more slowly and outer regions can grow rapidly. To achieve a dual microstructure the authors recommend holding time should be less than 60 min. They note a treatment window where bore <1110°C and rim 1130–1150°C yields fine/coarse region grain grades (bore ~9–10, rim ~5.5–6). Heat transfer in their model included convection and radiation between disk, furnace wall and furnace air. Monte Carlo simulation was used to model grain growth. These points inform soak temperature selection relative to solvus, the importance of radial temperature control and limiting hold time to reduce undesirable grain growth during homogenization/stress-relief.


---

## pqac-00000005

From text 'mohrbacher2026applicationofmicroalloying pages 4-6'.

Hardy Mohrbacher. Application of microalloying for controlling recrystallization and grain growth during downstream steel processing. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:2548-2571, Mar 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5, doi:10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt provides annealing guidance relevant to controlling recovery, recrystallization and grain growth in steels that can inform vacuum annealing schedules. It emphasizes using the Hollomon–Jaffe (HP) parameter to combine temperature and time into a single metric (HP = T·[log(t)+20]·10^-3 as presented) and reports empirical HP ranges: recovery for HP ≈ 16–19.3, partial microstructures in the intermediate range, and full recrystallization and grain growth when HP > 20.5. Microalloying (especially solute niobium and molybdenum) strongly retards recovery/recrystallization and widens the stable recovery window, while precipitation (e.g., 50–60 pct of available Nb) or raising peak temperatures to promote precipitation reduces solute drag and can permit grain growth. Practical observations include that 0.04 wt% Nb steel required ~80× longer at 700 °C to reach 95% recrystallization, and that in continuous lines short cycle times (≈120 s) may force peak temperatures toward ~800 °C. Lower coiling/soak temperatures retain microalloy in solution and give finer grains; the achievable recrystallized grain size limit is ≈3 µm. Overall, select soak T–t to keep HP below recrystallization thresholds, coordinate with alloy solute/precipitation behavior, and use microalloying/precipitation control to prevent unwanted grain growth.


---

## pqac-00000006

From text 'seo2020effectofmo pages 1-2'.

Eun Jung Seo, John G. Speer, David K. Matlock, and Robert L. Cryderman. Effect of mo in combination with nb on austenite grain size control in vacuum carburizing steels. Apr 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11665-020-04751-8, doi:10.1007/s11665-020-04751-8. This article has 24 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt discusses vacuum carburizing at elevated temperatures (up to ~1100°C) to shorten cycle times and increase productivity, but it warns that higher carburizing temperatures promote austenite grain growth, which can degrade fatigue performance. To limit austenite grain coarsening during carburizing, microalloying to form small carbo-nitride precipitates is effective. Nb additions alone provide some control of abnormal austenite grain growth, while Mo combined with Nb gives enhanced resistance to grain growth, particularly at high carburizing temperatures up to ~1050°C; this enhanced control is attributed to solute and precipitation effects. The paper also describes two pre-heat treatment approaches: (1) austenitizing with isothermal annealing to alter ferrite/pearlite morphology and (2) a subcritical anneal to soften by annealing ferrite and bainite. The excerpt contrasts conventional gas carburizing temperatures (920–940°C) with vacuum carburizing and notes vacuum processing advantages (reduced intergranular oxidation, cleaner surfaces, compressed gas quenching) and that compressed gas quenching may require higher hardenability. These points are relevant to strategies for avoiding grain growth during high-temperature vacuum furnace treatments.


---

## pqac-00000007

From text 'mohrbacher2026applicationofmicroalloying pages 1-2'.

Hardy Mohrbacher. Application of microalloying for controlling recrystallization and grain growth during downstream steel processing. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:2548-2571, Mar 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5, doi:10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt reviews how microalloying (notably niobium, titanium, vanadium, molybdenum) influences recovery, recrystallization, phase transformation, and grain growth during downstream thermal processing. Key points relevant to annealing and grain-growth control: thermal cycle durations used downstream span seconds to tens of hours and heating/cooling rates can be extremely fast or very slow, so soak times and rates strongly affect microalloy state. Precipitation in hot austenite is relatively slow and follows a C‑curve with a nose around 900–930 °C, meaning time/temperature windows exist where precipitate nucleation/growth is most rapid. Ti and Nb are strong carbide/nitride formers with low solubility, and several microalloy atoms are larger than iron, promoting segregation to boundaries/dislocations; these attributes (plus solute drag and precipitate pinning quantified by the SRP/TNR concepts) retard recrystallization and grain growth. Deformation accelerates particle nucleation, altering precipitate evolution during subsequent anneals. These mechanistic observations imply that controlling soak temperature relative to precipitation kinetics, tailoring heating/cooling rates, and leveraging microalloy chemistry/precipitates are central strategies to achieve stress relief/homogenization while preventing excessive grain growth.


---

## pqac-00000008

From text 'mohrbacher2026applicationofmicroalloying pages 2-4'.

Hardy Mohrbacher. Application of microalloying for controlling recrystallization and grain growth during downstream steel processing. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:2548-2571, Mar 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5, doi:10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt discusses how annealing temperature and time control precipitation, solute drag, and grain-boundary pinning in microalloyed steels, which is directly relevant to stress-relief/homogenization and grain-growth control. It notes that precipitation kinetics accelerate at higher annealing temperatures—"completeness of precipitation is achieved in a shorter time period at higher annealing temperature"—and that particle size grows with longer isothermal holds, especially "when annealing above the Curie temperature due to the significantly better diffusivity of niobium in paramagnetic ferrite." Smaller second-phase particles are more effective at Zener pinning ("For the same second-phase volume fraction, smaller particle sizes are more effective in pinning grain-boundary motion"), while elongated particles can be particularly effective at retarding grain growth ("second-phase particles with an elongated morphology are more effective in retarding grain growth"). For austenite, microalloy precipitation is slow and follows a C-curve with a nose near 900–930 C, so precipitates often do not reach completeness during industrial austenite conditioning. The text also emphasizes the role of solute drag ("solute drag has a relatively more significant contribution to austenite conditioning") and that the overall "time–temperature schedule ... determines the microstructural features." These points inform decisions on soak temperature/time trade-offs: higher temperature shortens required soak for precipitation and homogenization but promotes coarsening/grain growth unless pinned by fine or elongated second-phase particles or by solute drag; alloy content and process (batch vs continuous) strongly affect outcomes.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000009

From text 'digges1966heattreatmentand pages 36-37'.

Thomas G Digges and Samuel J Rosenberg. Heat treatment and properties of iron and steel. ArXiv, Oct 1966. URL: https://doi.org/10.6028/nbs.mono.18, doi:10.6028/nbs.mono.18. This article has 94 citations.

The provided excerpt contains Table 6, a catalog of recommended heat treatments for many AISI/SAE steels, including a dedicated "Annealing temperature" column with temperature ranges given in °F for individual grades (examples: 1040: 1,476–1,526°F; 4140: 1,500–1,550°F; 4340: 1,600–1,650°F). The table is oriented to heat-treating grades and pairs normalizing, annealing, hardening temperatures and suggested quenching media. Accompanying notes state that omitted steels can be estimated by interpolation and that the upper end of the heat-treating ranges should be used for large sections. The excerpt does not explicitly label anneals as "stress-relief" versus "homogenization," nor does it provide soak times per inch of thickness or specific guidance for stainless steels or superalloys. Quench-media guidance and section-size considerations are present, but explicit soak-time rules and annealing classifications (stress relief/homogenization) for tool steels, carbon steels, stainless steels, and superalloys are not included in this excerpt.

  In this 100th anniversary year of ASM International, it is especially ﬁtting to release ASM Handbook,
Volume 4A, Steel Heat Treating Fundamentals and Processes. Since its 1913 origin as the Steel Treaters
Club, formed by Detroit blacksmith William Park Woodside, ASM International has grown in scope; yet
steel heat treating remains a core subject of the Society. Woodside’s vision and recognition of the need to
exchange information on steel heat treating are further recognized by many successful publications
including the renowned Metals Handbook.

  The ASM Handbook (formerly Metals Handbook) series is being expanded into several volumes on
heat treatment. This reﬂects the roots of ASM International, as well as the Heat Treating Society (An
Afﬁliate Society of ASM International) with its ongoing member contributions in the ﬁeld of heat treat-
ing. ASM International and the Heat Treating Society extend a very special thanks to George E. Totten
and Jon Dossett as Volume Editors. Their initiatives and contributions were instrumental in the develop-
ment of this Volume. We are indebted to them and to the subject editors, authors, and reviewers for this
publication.

Abstract:
In the present work, we introduce a state parameter-based microstructure evolution model, which incorporates the effect of solute atoms and precipitates on recrystallization kinetics. The model accounts for local precipitate coarsening at grain boundaries, which promotes an average grain boundary movement even if the Zener pinning force exceeds the driving force for recrystallization. The impact of solute drag on the grain boundary mobility as well as simultaneous precipitation is discussed in detail. The model is validated on experimental data on recrystallization in V-micro-alloyed steel, where excellent agreement is achieved.

Abstract:
A mean-field model has been developed to simulate curvature-driven grain growth by exploring the evolution of grain size distribution under arbitrary thermal histories. The model was integrated into precipitation module TC-PRISMA, so that the pinning effect of the concurrently precipitated particles on the growing grains can be considered by a modified, location-specific Zener model. The developed model was validated against analytical calculations, and then applied to real alloy systems, fed with assessed grain boundary energy and mobility data. Its capabilities, limitations, and directions to improvements have been discussed.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000013

From text 'kisko2015effectofnb pages 6-7'.

Anna Kisko, Juho Talonen, David Arthur Porter, and Leo Pentti Karjalainen. Effect of nb microalloying on reversion and grain growth in a high-mn 204cu austenitic stainless steel. Isij International, 55:2217-2224, Oct 2015. URL: https://doi.org/10.2355/isijinternational.isijint-2015-156, doi:10.2355/isijinternational.isijint-2015-156. This article has 29 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt discusses Zener-type pinning of austenite grain boundaries by Nb-containing precipitates and the roles of particle size (r) and volume fraction (f). Only particles <50 nm were used for pinning-force calculations because these fine precipitates are effective at boundary pinning. Theoretical calculations assume all Nb is in precipitates (NbC or NbN). Driving forces (Fd) were evaluated using Zener and Gladman expressions (Fd = 2γ/D form) and pinning forces (Fp) using Zener-based models in rigid boundary (RBM) and flexible boundary (FBM) forms; the equations show Fp scales with f, γ and inversely with r (different prefactors for Eqs. (5)–(7)). Parameters used include γ = 0.8 J/m2 and size advantage Z = 2. TEM-measured mean radii (Table 3) and volume fractions (Table 4) were used (e.g. for 0.28Nb f ≈ 0.29/0.26/0.308; for 0.45Nb f ≈ 0.50/0.50/0.495). Comparisons of Fd and Fp indicate that higher Nb (≈0.28 wt.% and above) yields sufficient pinning (especially in FBM) to prevent grain coarsening at 1373 K, while lower Nb steels show some grain growth. Equating Fd and Fp implies a limiting grain size that increases with particle radius and decreases with increasing particle volume fraction (D_lim roughly ∝ r/f).


---

## pqac-00000014

From text 'sha2009graingrowthbehavior pages 3-6'.

Qingyun Sha and Zuqing Sun. Grain growth behavior of coarse-grained austenite in a nb-v-ti microalloyed steel. Materials Science and Engineering A-structural Materials Properties Microstructure and Processing, 523:77-84, Oct 2009. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msea.2009.05.037, doi:10.1016/j.msea.2009.05.037. This article has 107 citations.

The authors evaluate Zener-type pinning of austenite grain boundaries by precipitates using several models (Zener, rigid boundary model (RBM), flexible boundary model (FBM)) and driving-force expressions (Zener and Gladman). Assumptions used include Gladman Z = 2 and an interfacial energy of 0.8 J/m2; a maximum grain size of 1600 µm was used for driving-force calculations. Because Ti-rich carbonitrides are rectangular rather than spherical, an equivalent radius was computed to use spherical-particle expressions, and precipitate volume fraction was estimated by thermodynamic calculation. Calculations show FBM predicts larger pinning forces than Zener or RBM (more particles interacting with boundaries). Comparing pinning (Fp) and driving (Fd) forces indicates driving forces exceed pinning only for smaller grains (<600 µm); for larger grains pinning forces are larger (values close to each other), implying an equilibrium between grain coarsening and precipitate coarsening. The strong pinning by Ti-rich carbonitrides below 1250 ◦C inhibits grain growth (fine and coarse austenite) and abnormal grain growth would be expected only at higher temperatures.


---

## pqac-00000015

From text 'ringer1990oninteractionsand pages 77-83'.

On interactions and reactions between migrating grain and phase boundaries, and precipitate particles This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt discusses Zener-type pinning of austenite grain boundaries by second-phase particles, emphasizing that limiting grain size during annealing is controlled by particle volume fraction (f) and mean particle size (R). Maximum grain refinement is achieved with a high volume fraction and the smallest possible mean particle size; particles formed at lower temperatures (solid-state precipitation) are most effective, while those formed during solidification tend to be coarser and less effective. Simulations and analyses reported show a stronger inhibition of grain growth than classic Zener/Smith random-interaction predictions, with the fraction of particle–boundary interactions and the dependence of pinned grain size on f being larger than 2D expectations. TiN in microalloyed steels is given as a case study: TiN precipitates as fine, stable cuboids with low solubility, making it particularly effective at pinning and resistant to coarsening. Particle coarsening during thermal exposure follows the Lifshitz–Wagner kinetics, so lower dissolved solute (e.g., [Ti]) and faster cooling reduce coarsening and improve pinning efficacy. Overall, smaller R, larger f, low solute levels and minimized coarsening give the smallest limiting grain size.


---

## pqac-00000016

From text 'wu2022meanfieldmodeling pages 6-7'.

Kaisheng Wu, Johan Jeppsson, and Paul Mason. Mean field modeling of grain growth and zener pinning. Journal of Phase Equilibria and Diffusion, 43:866-875, Nov 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11669-022-01005-z, doi:10.1007/s11669-022-01005-z. This article has 15 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt discusses numerical mean-field modeling of particle pinning (Zener pinning) and its effect on limiting grain size during annealing. Zener parameters (K and m) from Hillert (K = 0.222, m = 0.93) were used to compute a Zener radius (about 8 lm), but numerical results indicate adjustments are needed because the simulated ratio of Rm to RCr (~0.5–0.6) is lower than the 0.75 assumed in prior theory. For the special case Rm = 0.5 RCr, the model predicts RCr = Rz (the Zener radius). Simulations show strong dependence on initial grain size: small initial grains (0.2 lm) produce discontinuous, stepwise growth with multiple plateaus as precipitates nucleate/coarsen and pin grain boundaries; larger initial grains (3.2 lm) show continuous but slowed growth and deviation from parabolic kinetics. The model uses an Arrhenius grain-boundary mobility (M = M0 exp(-Q/RT)) and a calibrated mobility giving ~7.94e-16 m4/Js at 722 °C. Overall, particle size, volume fraction (via Zener radius), and initial grain size control whether growth is arrested near the Zener radius or continues with reduced kinetics.


---

## pqac-00000017

From text 'ringer1990oninteractionsand pages 167-174'.

On interactions and reactions between migrating grain and phase boundaries, and precipitate particles This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt gives a Zener-type relationship for the limiting grain size D set by precipitate pinning: D = (1/K) · d / f (equation 9.9), i.e. limiting grain size scales directly with particle size d and inversely with particle volume fraction f, with K an efficiency factor. K depends on particle shape, crystallographic orientation and particle-matrix coherency: cubic particles generally exert larger drag than spheres, and coherent precipitates provide the highest resistance to boundary migration. For spheres a form used is K = 3.(1 + cos y0)/2. The pressures in Table 9.1 are maximum values based on assumptions (all particles similarly oriented) and an approximation for surface density; orientation distributions should be averaged if present. The authors note the coherency degree alters pinning (plots shown in figures 9.5–9.6) and that the quantitative equations could be improved with better statistical treatment, but the approximations give reasonable order-of-magnitude results. Measurements discussed concern Ti-microalloyed steel prior-austenite grains.


---

## pqac-00000018

From text 'ringer1990oninteractionsand pages 90-95'.

On interactions and reactions between migrating grain and phase boundaries, and precipitate particles This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt discusses Zener (Zener/Smith) pinning of austenite grain boundaries by TiN precipitates and emphasizes that both particle volume fraction and particle size strongly control limiting grain size during annealing. Experimental data (Pickering) show clear effects of TiN volume fraction and particle size on austenite grain size. Maintaining small, coarsening-resistant TiN particles requires low dissolved Ti in austenite and thus relatively high dissolved N at a given austenitising temperature; this condition is favored by a hypostoichiometric composition. Rapid solidification (e.g., continuous casting) refines TiN formed during solidification, permitting more hypostoichiometry. However, predictability of limiting grain size using a simple Zener/Smith relation is limited because particle shape (non-spherical), coarsening behavior, interactions with other microalloying elements (V, Nb), and solute levels complicate the microstructure. Related models for recrystallization (Cotterill) show that reducing interparticle spacing transitions behavior from accelerated to severely retarded recrystallization, indicating that pinning pressure and kinetics depend on spacing, size, and particle distribution.


---

## pqac-00000019

From text 'ringer1989ontheinteraction pages 5-7'.

S.P. Ringer, W.B. Li, and K.E. Easterling. On the interaction and pinning of grain boundaries by cubic shaped precipitate particles. Acta Metallurgica, 37:831-841, Mar 1989. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/0001-6160(89)90010-2, doi:10.1016/0001-6160(89)90010-2. This article has 142 citations.

Pages 5–7 discuss how cubic precipitates interact with migrating grain boundaries and how that interaction affects Zener-type pinning. The total system energy (particle plus boundary) depends on particle orientation; as a boundary moves it changes grain boundary area and the inclusion–matrix interface energy (coherent to incoherent), producing a rotation moment (force) on cubic particles. The paper gives a general relationship for that moment (for changes in the particle orientation angle) and notes the maximum moments are typically positive, so boundaries tend to rotate particles to increase the orientation angle. The magnitude of the moment depends on interaction angles, interfacial energy change, and coherency. Experimentally, the authors tested new Zener pinning equations using Ti-microalloyed and 321 austenitic steels with controlled isothermal and short anneals; particle size, volume fraction, and prior austenitic grain size were measured. Volume fractions were measured from thin-foil TEM techniques (known foil thickness, fringe/extinction methods) and extraction replicas, mean particle size from ~1000-particle statistics, and grain size by optical microscopy with ASTM linear intercept and planimetric analysis. These measurements were used to evaluate limiting grain size vs particle size/volume fraction in annealing.


---

## pqac-00000020

From text 'mohrbacher2026applicationofmicroalloying pages 2-4'.

Hardy Mohrbacher. Application of microalloying for controlling recrystallization and grain growth during downstream steel processing. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:2548-2571, Mar 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5, doi:10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt highlights factors relevant to Zener pinning during annealing. It states that for a given second-phase volume fraction, smaller precipitate sizes are more effective at pinning grain-boundary motion, and that elongated particle morphologies provide stronger retardation of grain growth. Coherent particle/matrix interfaces can increase pinning potency through interfacial and misfit-generated elastic energies. Precipitate size grows with isothermal hold time and more rapidly at higher annealing temperatures (notably above the Curie point for ferrite), while precipitation in austenite is relatively slow (C-curve with a nose around 900–930 °C) and can be substantially suppressed depending on coiling/annealing strategy. NbC particles typically show an aspect ratio ~2–2.5 and may change morphology (e.g., to more spherical and incoherent upon transformation), which also affects their pinning effectiveness. These observations collectively bear on how particle size, volume fraction, morphology, interface coherence, and processing thermal history control the limiting grain size via Zener-type pinning during annealing.

                    <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                    grain evolution take place quickly during the heating step to the solution temperature and is most likely governed by static recrystallization, where the final grain size distribution is defined by the density of recrystallized grain nuclei whose boundaries migrate to consume the residual work hardened grains in the microstructure. Microstructure with limited work hardening (

                    m) and rather heterogeneous grain size distribution. Additional interrupted SHTs, microstructural investigations and rough calculations of the driving pressures exerted on the grain boundaries illustrated that such a phenomenon could be favored by several concomitant factors during the 2 °C/min temperature ramp up to the SHT temperature, such as ripening of the
                    <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>

    url = "https://doi.org/10.22226/2410-3535-2018-4-494-498",
    publisher = "Institute for Metals Superplasticity Problems of RAS",
    issue = "4",
    issn = "2218-5046"
}


Abstract:
The microstructure and mechanical properties of a novel nickel base superalloy intended for manufacturing of die tools and parts for gas turbine engine have been investigated. The superalloy designated as SLZhS-1R had the following composition: Ni-47(Al,Cr,Co,Ta,W,Hf)-0.2(C,B) (wt. %). The as-cast superalloy was subjected to homogenization and heterogenization heat treatment, which is typically performed before hot working. However, the heat treatment led to appearance of platelike precipitates, which are not favorable for the hot workability. Therefore, the superalloy was subjected to hot working taking the as-cast condition as the initial one. To do it, unidirectional hot forging at a temperature slightly below the γ' solvus temperature with a strain rate of ε' ~ 10−2 s−1 was carried out. The as-cast superalloy showed reasonable workability that allowed producing sound forgings. The forging procedure provided formation of mixed microstructure consisted of coarse non-recrystallized and fine recrystallized γ grains. EBSD analysis of the forged workpiece showed that the fraction of highangle grain boundaries was insignificant. The hot forging resulted in dynamic recovery, whereas recrystallization processes occurred only locally near the γ grain boundaries. Tensile tests were carried out for the heat treated and hot forged conditions as well as for the as-cast condition. The heat treated and especially the forged and heat treated condition showed appreciably higher strength and ductility as compared to those of the cast condition. The obtained tensile properties were compared with those of the widely applied Russian superalloy ZhS6U.
