Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000021

From text 'andersson2011weldabilityofprecipitation pages 41-44'.

J Andersson. Weldability of precipitation hardening superalloys: influence of microstructure. Unknown journal, 2011.

The excerpt gives practical heat-treatment conditions used for homogenization, solution (sub‑/super‑solvus) treatments and grain growth for Ni superalloys (Alloy 718, Allvac 718Plus and Waspaloy). Furnace heating was 0.3 °C/s with enforced argon cooling to 500 °C and cooling rates >0.3 °C/min. For homogenization of cast Allvac 718Plus the study used 1125 °C-5 h (below γ‑Laves eutectic), 1200 °C-5 h (above eutectic), and a combined 1125 °C-5 h + 1200 °C-5 h. For Alloy 718, short/standard solution treatments were 954 °C-1 h and 982 °C-1 h (giving different amounts of δ‑phase), while long exposure at 954 °C-15 h produces large amounts of δ. Large grain sizes were produced by treatments such as 1050 °C-3 h + 954 °C-1 h and 1020 °C-1 h. Allvac 718Plus used repeated short solution steps (954 °C-0.5 h, repeated 3×) for stress relief. For Waspaloy, solution heat treatments of 996–1080 °C for 4 h were used, with the higher temperatures producing larger grain size. These documented temperatures and dwell times provide practical starting points for homogenization, sub‑/super‑solvus processing and controlled grain growth during annealing.


---

## pqac-00000022

From text 'strady2026grainstructurebehavior pages 1-2'.

C. Strady, A. Nicolay, M. Bignon, A. Agnoli, J. de Jaeger, N. Bozzolo, and M. Bernacki. Grain structure behavior of powder metallurgy nickel base γ–γ′ superalloys under γ′-supersolvus solution heat-treatment. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:1872-1883, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-025-08107-6, doi:10.1007/s11661-025-08107-6. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt reports practical, alloy-level observations for powder-metallurgy Ni-based c–c¢ superalloys. The c¢ solvus is given as Tc¢–s. between 1100 C and 1160 C; solution heat treatment (SHT) above this solvus produces a uniform c-grain structure ~20–40 µm. Service requirements contrast bore (fine-grained, 5–10 µm) and rim (coarse-grained, 50–80 µm). c-grain evolution occurs rapidly during heating to the solution temperature and is likely governed by static recrystallization; the final grain-size distribution is set by the density of recrystallized nuclei whose boundaries consume residual work-hardened grains. Materials with limited residual work hardening can develop heterogeneous structures with abnormal grains >200 µm, which reduce fatigue resistance. Nanometric oxide ((Hf, Zr)O2) and carbide ((Nb, Ti)C) particles exert pinning pressure that stabilizes grain structure once work hardening is removed. A slower heating rate (notably the 2 C/min ramp cited) increases the likelihood of excessive grain growth on slightly work-hardened material — possibly due to ripening/dissolution of c¢ precipitates during ramp-up. Cooling rate from SHT controls subsequent c¢ precipitation (sub-solvus aging).


---

## pqac-00000023

From text 'tian2009experimentalandsimulation pages 6-7'.

Gaofeng Tian, Chengchang Jia, Jiantao Liu, and Benfu Hu. Experimental and simulation on the grain growth of p/m nickel-base superalloy during the heat treatment process. Materials & Design, 30:433-439, Mar 2009. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007, doi:10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007. This article has 71 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The paper reports practical heat-treatment conditions and simulated effects on grain growth for a PM nickel-base superalloy. They set furnace temperature ~20–40 °C above the c0 solvus (1150 C used). Transient FEM simulations examined a 1150 C treatment with holding times of 15–120 min; local point temperatures converge with time. At 60 min, bore points A–C were <1110 C while rim points D–F were 1110–1145 C; inner regions (A–C) showed slow grain growth, outer regions (E–F) rapid growth. To obtain a dual microstructure the authors recommend holding time <60 min. Monte Carlo grain-growth simulation produced region-specific grain-size grades: A,B,C = 9–10; D = 7.5–8; E = 6–6.5; F = 5.5–6. They state that keeping the disk bore temperature below ~1110 C while maintaining rim temperatures ~1130–1150 C yields coarse-grained bore (grade ~9–10) and finer rim (~5.5–6). The study emphasizes temperature gradients and hold time control for sub-/super-solvus style dual microstructure and grain growth management.


---

## pqac-00000024

From text 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 2-5'.

Yan-Xing Liu, Zhi-Jiang Ke, Run-Hua Li, Ju-Qing Song, and Jing-Jing Ruan. Study of grain growth in a ni-based superalloy by experiments and cellular automaton model. Materials, 14:6922, Nov 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/ma14226922, doi:10.3390/ma14226922. This article has 16 citations.

The study used a solution heat treatment of 1338 K for 1200 s followed by an ice-water quench to fully dissolve precipitates before grain-growth testing. Isothermal grain-growth experiments were performed at 1353–1473 K with holding times from 180 to 2400 s. The initial average grain size after solution treatment was ≈70 µm. Grain size increases with both holding time and temperature: at 1353 K the grain sizes at 600, 1500 and 2400 s were 106, 154 and 192 µm respectively; at a 540 s hold, grain sizes at 1413, 1443 and 1473 K were 160, 238 and 299 µm respectively. Grain growth follows a parabolic-type law G^n_t − G^n_0 = k_G t with k_G = k0 exp(−Q/RT). The authors identified n = 2.43 and fitted k0 and Q such that G2.43_t − G2.43_0 = 3.63 × 10^14 exp[−3.2 × 10^5/(RT)] t. Twin boundaries were merged for average-size evaluation and size was computed by an area-fraction-weighted method.


---

## pqac-00000025

From text 'tian2009experimentalandsimulation pages 1-2'.

Gaofeng Tian, Chengchang Jia, Jiantao Liu, and Benfu Hu. Experimental and simulation on the grain growth of p/m nickel-base superalloy during the heat treatment process. Materials & Design, 30:433-439, Mar 2009. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007, doi:10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007. This article has 71 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

This study on a powder-metallurgy nickel-base superalloy reports solution (heat) treatment in the range 950–1150 °C for 5–60 min with water quenching. A gradient/controlled solution treatment produced a dual microstructure (fine-grain bore grade ~9–10 and coarse-grain rim grade ~5.5–6). At 1010 °C grain growth with holding time is slow: grain size increased only ~4 μm when holding time increased from 5 to 45 min. Grain growth accelerates markedly between ~1110–1150 °C; at 1150 °C the grain size reached about 43 μm (grade ~5.5). Specimens were prepared from PREP powder (50–100 μm), HIP consolidated, then hot-deformed at 1090 °C (strain rate ≈5×10⁻² s⁻¹, strain =0.7) to produce a fine-grained starting microstructure before solution treatment. Grain sizes were measured by optical microscopy (image analysis). These experimental results give practical anchor points: slow growth near ~1010 °C over tens of minutes, rapid growth above ~1110 °C, and substantial coarsening by 1150 °C. The data are specific to the alloy chemistry and processing route used here (Table 1).


---

## pqac-00000026

From text 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 1-2'.

Yan-Xing Liu, Zhi-Jiang Ke, Run-Hua Li, Ju-Qing Song, and Jing-Jing Ruan. Study of grain growth in a ni-based superalloy by experiments and cellular automaton model. Materials, 14:6922, Nov 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/ma14226922, doi:10.3390/ma14226922. This article has 16 citations.

This study reports experimental and modelling data on grain growth in a commercial Ni-based superalloy. A solution (homogenization) treatment used in the experiments was 1338 K for 1200 s to dissolve precipitates. Isothermal anneals for grain growth were performed between 1353 and 1473 K with holding times from 180 to 2400 s; the grain structure continuously coarsened as holding time and temperature increased. Grain growth followed a near-parabolic law (exponent slightly above 2), indicating boundary-migration-controlled kinetics with only minor pinning by precipitates. The authors found that grain-boundary mobility increases with temperature. A cellular automaton model was developed that reproduces the dependence of grain size on holding time and temperature. The paper also cites literature noting precipitate pinning (e.g., NbC and TiN) can retard growth and that boundary morphology at high temperature influences growth behavior. These experimental temperature/time ranges and kinetic observations provide concrete reference points for homogenization and annealing-driven grain growth in a Ni-based superalloy.


---

## pqac-00000027

From text 'chavilian2025acceleratedhomogenizationof pages 2-4'.

H Chavilian and SH Razavi. Accelerated homogenization of a cast nickel-based superalloy vdm780 via thermomechanical processing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt reports thermomechanical homogenization conditions used for the cast nickel-based superalloy VDM780. STA analysis guided temperature selection, and a preheating/homogenization temperature of 1160 °C was chosen as suitable to eliminate the as-cast structure and as the inter-pass preheat. During rolling the workpiece was maintained roughly in the 1080–1120 °C range (accounting for a typical 50–60 °C drop when removed from the furnace). Heating to study heat-treatment behavior used a 10 °C/min ramp up to 1300 °C. The thermomechanical cycle involved five rolling passes on a two-high mill at a constant strain rate of 4 s⁻¹; after each pass the sample was returned to the furnace within ~10 s to encourage post-dynamic recrystallization, and samples were air-cooled after the final pass. The paper emphasizes elimination of interdendritic as-cast phases (Nb-enriched, γ/Laves-like) via homogenization. The excerpt does not provide explicit isothermal hold times for homogenization, nor general sub-solvus vs super-solvus temperature ranges or quantitative grain-growth rates during annealing, so direct broader recommendations beyond the VDM780 conditions above are not given.

I.J. Moore, J.I. Taylor, M.W. Tracy, M.G. Burke, and E.J. Palmiere. Grain coarsening behaviour of solution annealed alloy 625 between 600–800 °c. Materials Science and Engineering A-structural Materials Properties Microstructure and Processing, 682:402-409, Jan 2017. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msea.2016.11.060, doi:10.1016/j.msea.2016.11.060. This article has 67 citations.

The provided excerpt is largely a bibliography pointing to studies directly relevant to homogenization, sub‑solvus/super‑solvus heat treatments, and grain growth in nickel superalloys—especially Alloy 625. Key items include a focused study on "Grain coarsening behaviour of solution annealed alloy 625 between 600–800 °c," work on "Precipitation and recrystallization behavior of the nickel‑base alloy Inconel 625," and papers on "Microstructural evolutions during thermal aging of Alloy 625: Impact of temperature and forming process." Long‑time exposure effects are covered ("Effects of long-time exposure in Alloy 625 at 1200◦F 1400◦F and 1600◦F"), which overlap the upper range of annealing/aging temperatures. Foundational references on grain growth and boundary migration (Ralph; Higgins) and the ASTM E112 standard for grain size measurement are also cited. While the excerpt does not provide explicit procedural temperatures/times or step‑by‑step homogenization schedules, it identifies primary literature and standards that contain experimental data and discussions on temperature ranges (notably 600–800 °C and ~1200–1600 °F) and on mechanisms (precipitation, recrystallization, grain‑boundary migration) that control grain growth during annealing.

## 2. Effects of Vacuum Annealing on the Conduction Characteristics of ZnO Nanosheets
Valid Text Names:
'barnett2015effectsofvacuum pages 1-2', 'barnett2015effectsofvacuum pages 2-5', 'barnett2015effectsofvacuum pages 5-6', 'barnett2015effectsofvacuum pages 5-5'

BibTex:
@article{barnett2015effectsofvacuum,
    author = "Barnett, Chris J. and Smith, Nathan A. and Jones, Daniel R. and Maffeis, Thierry G. G. and Cobley, Richard J.",
    title = "Effects of Vacuum Annealing on the Conduction Characteristics of ZnO Nanosheets",
    year = "2015",
    journal = "Nanoscale Research Letters",
    volume = "10",
    month = "Sep",
    doi = "10.1186/s11671-015-1066-1",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1186/s11671-015-1066-1",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "1931-7573"
}


Abstract:
ZnO nanosheets are a relatively new form of nanostructure and have demonstrated potential as gas-sensing devices and dye sensitised solar cells. For integration into other devices, and when used as gas sensors, the nanosheets are often heated. Here we study the effect of vacuum annealing on the electrical transport properties of ZnO nanosheets in order to understand the role of heating in device fabrication. A low cost, mass production method has been used for synthesis and characterisation is achieved using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), photoluminescence (PL), auger electron spectroscopy (AES) and nanoscale two-point probe. Before annealing, the measured nanosheet resistance displayed a non-linear increase with probe separation, attributed to surface contamination. Annealing to 300 °C removed this contamination giving a resistance drop, linear probe spacing dependence, increased grain size and a reduction in the number of n-type defects. Further annealing to 500 °C caused the n-type defect concentration to reduce further with a corresponding increase in nanosheet resistance not compensated by any further sintering. At 700 °C, the nanosheets partially disintegrated and the resistance increased and became less linear with probe separation. These effects need to be taken into account when using ZnO nanosheets in devices that require an annealing stage during fabrication or heating during use.

function.arguments: {"question": "Vacuum induction furnace for melting and remelting steels and superalloys, vacuum level and contamination control during heat treatment", "text_name_focus_list": ["meng2023designofvacuum pages 1-2", "meng2023designofvacuum pages 2-4", "prasanna2020evaluationofproperties pages 1-2", "prasanna2020evaluationofproperties pages 2-3", "seo2020effectofmo pages 3-6"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000029

From text 'chavilian2025acceleratedhomogenizationof pages 2-4'.

H Chavilian and SH Razavi. Accelerated homogenization of a cast nickel-based superalloy vdm780 via thermomechanical processing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt reports laboratory melting of the VDM780 superalloy using a vacuum induction furnace (VIM) operated at about 10⁻³ mbar. The cast ingot composition was confirmed by spark optical emission spectroscopy. STA analysis (heating at 10 °C/min up to 1300 °C) guided selection of homogenization and preheating temperatures. A preheat/homogenization temperature of 1160 °C was chosen based on STA and to allow the sample to remain in the 1080–1120 °C range during rolling. Thermomechanical processing used an electric resistance furnace with laser-pyrometer temperature control; samples were rapidly (within ~10 s) returned to the furnace after deformation to promote post-dynamic recrystallization. The process avoided ESR and VAR remelting routes. During handling a typical exit temperature drop of ~50–60 °C was observed. The excerpt documents vacuum level, temperature control, transfer timing, and analytical checks (OES, STA), but does not provide detailed contamination-control measures (e.g., gas backfilling, getter use, crucible materials, residual gas analysis) or explicit procedures to limit pickup during heat treatment.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 4-7'.

Jintao Meng, Haitao Gao, Mixue Ruan, Hai Guo, Xiaojie Zhou, and Di Zhang. Design of vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on ga-fuzzy-pid algorithm. PLOS ONE, 18:e0293823, Nov 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0293823, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0293823. This article has 14 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This paper describes a vacuum annealing furnace (vertical internal heating) rather than an induction melting furnace, but provides useful quantitative and control-system details relevant to vacuum heat treatment. The reported working vacuum is 8 × 10–3 Pa. Typical annealing temperatures are given as about 600–1000°C (with process table entries up to 1600°C and a rated operating range 850–1400°C), target uniformity ±5°C and control accuracy ±3°C. Heat transfer in the furnace is dominated by radiation from heaters to workpieces, with some conductive and weak convective components. Heat-loss terms include conduction to furnace walls, thermal short-circuiting, and losses via instrumentation/vacuum penetrations: “heat exported from thermocouples and vacuum tubes, as well as the dissipation of heat from the observation holes.” Temperature measurement and control use multiple thermocouples (control/monitoring in three zones and nine around the workpiece area), PLC communication over RS485 to upper computer software, and a GA-optimized PID (GA-Fuzzy-PID) that outputs via PLC and a 4–20 mA link to a power regulator controlling thyristors. The paper focuses on temperature control; it notes potential leakage/heat-loss paths through penetrations that are also relevant to vacuum integrity and contamination control, but it does not provide detailed contamination-control procedures specific to induction melting/remelting.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 2-4'.

Jintao Meng, Haitao Gao, Mixue Ruan, Hai Guo, Xiaojie Zhou, and Di Zhang. Design of vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on ga-fuzzy-pid algorithm. PLOS ONE, 18:e0293823, Nov 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0293823, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0293823. This article has 14 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes a vacuum annealing furnace temperature-control system that emphasizes coordinating vacuum level and temperature to improve process outcomes and reduce contamination, energy use, and pollution. The authors propose a GA-optimized fuzzy-PID controller to tune kp, ki and kd for better temperature control. Process steps require placing the workpiece, closing the furnace, evacuating the chamber, and initiating heating only when the vacuum degree meets heating conditions. Temperature sensing uses K-type thermocouples (measurement range 0˚C–1300˚C) and typical annealing working temperatures are cited as 600–1000˚C (with furnace capability to 1200˚C). Hardware architecture centers on a Siemens S7–1200 PLC with DI/DO/AI/AO modules; setpoint temperature curves are sent from an upper computer to the PLC over RS485 using MODBUS. This information touches on vacuum initiation criteria, sensing and control hardware, and an optimization approach for PID tuning relevant to controlling contamination and thermal conditions during vacuum heat treatments.

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a table summarizing practical soak temperature and time guidance ranges for vacuum furnace annealing of steels and alloys, organized by alloy class and treatment objective (stress relief vs. full annealing vs. homogenization). Include columns for: Alloy Class, Treatment Objective, Soak Temperature Range (°C), Typical Soak Time, Grain Growth Risk Level, and Key Notes. Cover the following alloy classes:
1. Low/medium carbon steels - stress relief (~540-600°C) and full annealing (~800-900°C)  
2. Alloy steels (e.g., 4140, 4340) - stress relief and full annealing (~815-900°C)
3. Stainless steels (austenitic like 316L, PH like 17-4 PH) - stress relief and homogenization/solutionizing
4. Ni-based superalloys (e.g., Alloy 718, Waspaloy, PM alloys) - sub-solvus solution (~950-1050°C), super-solvus (~1100-1200°C), and homogenization
5. Include soak time guidance: typically 0.5-1 hr per inch of section thickness for steels, specific times from evidence for superalloys"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:

| Alloy Class | Treatment Objective | Soak Temperature Range (°C) | Typical Soak Time | Grain Growth Risk Level | Key Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|
| Low/medium carbon steels | Stress relief / process anneal | 540–650 | 0.5–1 h/in section thickness; often ~1 h minimum after equalization | Low | Prefer the lowest temperature that achieves stress relief; staying below Ac1 minimizes recrystallization and grain coarsening. Digges notes process annealing near ~650°C and lower stress-relief around ~540°C when no further cold work is intended; vacuum heating improves surface cleanliness but soak should still be limited to the minimum needed for uniform temperature (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031) |
| Low/medium carbon steels | Full anneal | 800–900 | 0.5–1 h/in after the part reaches temperature | Medium–High | Heat about 15–30°C above the critical range, then soak only long enough for thermal equalization; longer hold increases ferrite/austenite grain size. Use the lower end for small sections and the upper end for large sections (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000009) |
| Alloy steels (e.g., 4140) | Stress relief | 540–675 | 0.5–1 h/in section thickness | Low–Medium | Keep below Ac1 to avoid unnecessary phase transformation. Vacuum processing is advantageous where scale-free surfaces or low decarburization are required; grain growth risk remains modest unless hold is excessive (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000030) |
| Alloy steels (e.g., 4140) | Full anneal | 815–845 | 0.5–1 h/in after equalization | Medium | Digges tabulates ~816–843°C for 4140-class annealing. Use shortest effective soak because grain growth depends strongly on time and temperature (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000001) |
| Alloy steels (e.g., 4340) | Full anneal | 870–900 | 0.5–1 h/in after equalization | Medium–High | Digges tabulates ~871–899°C for 4340-class annealing. Large sections may need the high end of the range, but higher soak severity increases grain-coarsening tendency (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000001) |
| Austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 316L) | Stress relief / recovery anneal | 600–800 | ~0.5–2 h depending on section size and residual stress level | Low–Medium | Favor recovery-only schedules when dimensional stability is the goal. In heavily cold-worked stainless steels, higher heating rates suppress selective grain growth and produce finer recrystallized structures; slow heating raises grain-growth risk (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000030) |
| Austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 316L) | Recrystallization / solution anneal | 800–950 for strong recrystallization; ~1050 typical solutionizing window by practice | ~0.5–1 h for sheet/light section; thicker sections need longer equalization | Medium–High | SUS316L showed annealing between 1073–1223 K (800–950°C) with finer grains at higher heating rates; once recrystallization is complete, further time at temperature promotes grain growth. Use rapid heat-up and minimum hold (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002) |
| PH stainless steels (e.g., 17-4 PH) | Stress relief | 550–650 | ~1–4 h, depending on residual stress and section | Low | Stress relief can reduce residual stress with limited coarsening. Keep below solutionizing/homogenization temperatures if grain size must be preserved (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000030) |
| PH stainless steels (e.g., 17-4 PH) | Homogenization / solutionizing | ~1050–1150 | Solutionizing ~1 h; homogenization ~1150°C for >90 min in cited cast-alloy guidance | Medium–High | For 17-4 PH analogs, homogenization at 1150°C for >90 min followed by solutionizing at 1050°C reduces segregation, but carbide coarsening still occurs and full microchemical homogenization may remain incomplete below the carbide solvus (~1250°C) (pqac-00000003) |
| Ni-based superalloys (Alloy 718) | Sub-solvus solution / stress relief | 954–982 | ~1 h typical; repeated 0.5 h steps also reported | Medium | Alloy 718 schedules of 954°C/1 h and 982°C/1 h are documented; 954°C/15 h produced much larger δ-phase content, so long holds should be avoided unless intentionally precipitating. Good choice when stress relief or controlled sub-solvus solution is needed without aggressive grain coarsening (pqac-00000021) |
| Ni-based superalloys (Waspaloy) | Sub-solvus / solution treatment | 996–1080 | ~4 h | Medium–High | Waspaloy solution treatment in this range produced larger grains at the higher temperatures. Stay at the lowest temperature consistent with dissolution/homogenization targets (pqac-00000021) |
| Ni-based superalloys (PM alloys) | Sub-solvus solution | 950–1050 | ~5–60 min; often ≤45–60 min | Low–Medium below ~1010°C; Medium near 1050°C | PM superalloy data show slow grain growth near ~1010°C, with only small size increase from 5 to 45 min. This is the preferred regime when stress relief/homogenization is needed with minimal coarsening (pqac-00000025) |
| Ni-based superalloys (PM alloys) | Near-/super-solvus solution | 1100–1150 | Typically 15–60 min; keep <60 min when dual microstructure or limited coarsening is required | High | Grain growth accelerates sharply above ~1110°C. A furnace set ~20–40°C above the γ′ solvus and hold times beyond ~60 min caused rapid coarsening, especially in hotter regions; temperature uniformity is critical (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) |
| Ni-based superalloys (cast 718Plus / similar) | Homogenization | 1125–1200 | ~5 h per step; combined 1125°C/5 h + 1200°C/5 h also reported | High | Effective for reducing segregation, but grain growth and eutectic/Laves-related risks rise rapidly as temperature increases. Use only where chemistry requires it, and verify against incipient-melting limits and segregation state (pqac-00000021) |
| Ni-based superalloys (cast VDM780-like) | Homogenization / preheat | ~1160 | Alloy-specific; evidence supports the temperature but not a general soak time | High | VIM-produced VDM780 used ~10^-3 mbar melting and selected 1160°C from STA as a homogenization/preheat temperature. Good example that homogenization temperature should be tied to thermal analysis and segregation morphology, not a generic rule (pqac-00000029) |
| Ni-based superalloys (general grain-growth kinetics reference) | Isothermal anneal after solutionizing | 1080–1200 | 180–2400 s in experiments | Medium–Very High | Experimental Ni-superalloy grain growth rises strongly with both temperature and time: e.g., after solution treatment, grain size increased substantially between 1353–1473 K and 180–2400 s. This supports a best practice of shortest possible hold and avoiding unnecessary exposure above solvus (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000026) |


*Table: This table summarizes practical soak temperature and time ranges for vacuum furnace annealing of steels and nickel-based alloys, organized by alloy class and treatment objective. It also flags relative grain-growth risk and notes the main control levers needed to achieve stress relief or homogenization without excessive coarsening.*

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a table summarizing best practices for controlling grain growth during vacuum furnace annealing of steels and alloys. Include columns for: Strategy, Mechanism/Rationale, Practical Guidance, and Applicable Alloy Systems. Cover the following strategies:
1. Minimize soak temperature - use lowest temperature that achieves treatment objective
2. Minimize soak time - match soak time to section thickness using established rules
3. Control heating rate - faster heating rates produce finer recrystallized grains
4. Microalloying with Nb, Ti, V, Mo - Zener pinning by fine carbide/nitride precipitates
5. Hollomon-Jaffe parameter optimization - use HP to combine temperature and time effects
6. Sub-solvus vs super-solvus selection - stay below solvus when possible
7. Precipitate size and volume fraction control - smaller particles more effective at pinning
8. Vacuum level and temperature uniformity - maintain proper vacuum and minimize thermal gradients
9. Cooling rate control - manage precipitation during cooling"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| Strategy | Mechanism/Rationale | Practical Guidance | Applicable Alloy Systems |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimize soak temperature | Grain growth rate rises strongly with temperature because grain-boundary mobility increases; full anneals and near-/above-solvus treatments promote much more coarsening than recovery/stress-relief cycles (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000026) | Use the lowest temperature that still achieves the objective: for steel stress relief stay subcritical when possible; for stainless and Ni alloys prefer recovery or sub-solvus schedules unless segregation or phase-dissolution requirements force higher temperatures (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000025) | Carbon and alloy steels, stainless steels, PH stainless steels, Ni-based superalloys |
| Minimize soak time | Time at temperature adds directly to grain coarsening; even when temperature is fixed, longer holds increase grain size and can coarsen pinning precipitates (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025) | For steels, use only the time needed for thermal equalization, commonly about 0.5–1 h/in section thickness; for Ni alloys keep high-temperature holds as short as practical, often tens of minutes rather than hours near the solvus unless homogenization specifically requires longer exposure (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000023) | Steels, stainless steels, Ni-based superalloys |
| Control heating rate | Faster heating increases nucleation density during recrystallization and reduces the time spent in the temperature window where selective grain growth can occur; slow heating permits preferential growth of some grains (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000022) | Ramp as quickly as part geometry and distortion limits allow through the recrystallization range; avoid unnecessarily slow ramps for cold-worked stainless steels and PM superalloys, especially near solvus temperatures where abnormal grains can form during prolonged heat-up (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000022) | Cold-worked stainless steels, PM Ni superalloys, heavily deformed alloys |
| Microalloying with Nb, Ti, V, Mo | Fine carbides/nitrides/carbonitrides pin grain boundaries by Zener pinning and solute drag; Nb, Ti, V, and Mo improve resistance to abnormal grain growth, especially in austenite (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014) | Where chemistry can be selected, use Nb/Ti/V microalloying for steels and Nb+Mo combinations for high-temperature vacuum-carburizing or annealing steels; maintain precipitates fine and numerous rather than relying on coarse particles (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000020) | Microalloyed steels, vacuum-carburizing steels, austenitic stainless steels |
| Hollomon-Jaffe parameter optimization | Temperature and time act together; the Hollomon-Jaffe parameter provides a compact way to compare schedules and avoid drifting from recovery/stress relief into recrystallization and grain growth (pqac-00000005) | For microalloyed steels, keep the parameter in the recovery-dominant window when stress relief is the goal; avoid HP values associated with full recrystallization and grain growth unless refinement via recrystallization is desired (pqac-00000005) | Steels, especially microalloyed and cold-worked strip/product forms |
| Sub-solvus vs super-solvus selection | Below the solvus, undissolved precipitates remain to pin boundaries; above the solvus, pinning is lost and grain growth accelerates sharply (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000023) | Stay sub-solvus whenever stress relief or moderate homogenization is sufficient; move to near-/super-solvus only when segregation removal or phase dissolution requires it, and then strictly limit hold time and thermal gradients (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000023) | Ni-based superalloys, PH alloys, some stainless and tool steels with stable precipitates |
| Precipitate size and volume fraction control | Limiting grain size scales roughly with particle size divided by particle volume fraction; smaller, more numerous particles give stronger pinning, and elongated/coherent particles can be even more effective (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000020) | Design process routes to preserve fine precipitates: avoid overlong holds that coarsen them, exploit lower-temperature precipitation where feasible, and recognize that coarse solidification precipitates are usually less effective than fine solid-state precipitates (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000020) | Microalloyed steels, precipitation-strengthened stainless steels, Ni superalloys |
| Vacuum level and temperature uniformity | Vacuum limits oxidation/decarburization and contamination, but grain control still depends on uniform temperature; local hot spots and radial gradients produce localized rapid grain growth (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031) | Heat only after the target vacuum is achieved; maintain stable vacuum, good thermocouple coverage, and tight uniformity. Typical reported practice includes high vacuum for annealing and minimizing losses/leaks through penetrations. Avoid load arrangements that create rim-to-core temperature differences near critical temperatures (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000023) | All vacuum-annealed steels and alloys; especially large sections and PM/cast superalloys |
| Cooling rate control | Cooling affects re-precipitation, retained phase fractions, and post-soak coarsening; too slow a cool can allow unwanted precipitation or continued microstructural coarsening, while controlled cooling can preserve the intended sub-solvus/supersolvus result (pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022) | After high-temperature holds, cool at the rate required by the alloy system: enforce sufficiently fast cooling from solution temperatures in Ni alloys and PH stainless steels to prevent unwanted precipitation during descent, but tailor the cool to avoid distortion or cracking in steels (pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000003) | PH stainless steels, Ni-based superalloys, alloy steels |
| Tie cycle design to section size and segregation severity | Thick sections need more time to equalize, but excess dwell beyond equalization only increases coarsening; heavily segregated cast structures may justify harsher homogenization than wrought or PM materials (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000027) | Use mild stress-relief schedules for wrought parts and reserve long high-temperature homogenization cycles for castings or severely segregated material. Validate temperature selection with thermal analysis/solvus data rather than using one generic recipe across alloys (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000029) | Wrought steels, cast stainless steels, cast and wrought Ni-based superalloys |


*Table: This table summarizes evidence-based strategies for limiting grain coarsening during vacuum furnace annealing while still achieving stress relief or homogenization. It is useful as a process-planning checklist across steels, stainless steels, and Ni-based alloys.*

messages.content:
Updated plan:
{
  "objectives": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "objective": "Search for literature on vacuum induction furnace annealing of steels and alloys, focusing on grain growth control during stress relief and homogenization",
      "rationale": "Initial searches returned many papers. Now need to gather evidence from them.",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Found papers on vacuum annealing, grain growth kinetics, stress relief, homogenization of steels and superalloys",
      "evaluation": "Multiple relevant papers retrieved covering the topic"
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "objective": "Search for practical guidance on soak temperature and time ranges for vacuum annealing of steels and alloys",
      "rationale": "Found comprehensive data on temperature-time ranges for multiple alloy classes",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Found specific temperature-time data for carbon/alloy steels, stainless steels, and Ni-based superalloys from multiple sources",
      "evaluation": "Evidence covers stress relief (~540-650°C for steels), full annealing (~800-900°C for steels), homogenization (1100-1200°C for superalloys)"
    },
    {
      "id": 3,
      "objective": "Search for grain growth mechanisms and control strategies during high-temperature annealing",
      "rationale": "Understanding grain growth mechanisms is essential for providing best practices",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Found extensive evidence on Zener pinning, solute drag, microalloying effects (Nb, Ti, Mo), Hollomon-Jaffe parameter, heating rate effects, and precipitate-based grain boundary control",
      "evaluation": "Multiple high-quality papers provide mechanistic understanding and practical approaches"
    },
    {
      "id": 4,
      "objective": "Gather and synthesize evidence from retrieved papers",
      "rationale": "Sufficient evidence gathered across all key topics",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Comprehensive evidence collected on: (1) vacuum furnace annealing parameters and control, (2) stress relief and homogenization temperature-time ranges for steels and superalloys, (3) grain growth kinetics and mechanisms, (4) Zener pinning, solute drag, microalloying strategies, (5) heating rate effects on grain growth",
      "evaluation": "32 pieces of evidence from 18 relevant papers provide sufficient coverage"
    },
    {
      "id": 5,
      "objective": "Create artifacts summarizing key findings including temperature-time guidance table",
      "rationale": "A structured table will help present the practical guidance clearly",
      "status": "completed",
      "result": "Created three artifacts: (1) temperature-time guidance table, (2) best practices strategies table, (3) BibTeX reference list",
      "evaluation": "All key findings organized into clear, citable artifacts"
    },
    {
      "id": 6,
      "objective": "Submit final answer with citations and BibTeX references",
      "rationale": "User explicitly requests cited sources with BibTeX format",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    }
  ]
}

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor
query.title: Query

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

## 1. Design of vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on GA-Fuzzy-PID algorithm
Valid Text Names:
'meng2023designofvacuum pages 1-2', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 2-4', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 4-7', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 7-8', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 8-11', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 11-14', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 14-17', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 17-20', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 20-23', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 23-24', 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 24-25'

BibTex:
@article{meng2023designofvacuum,
    author = "Meng, Jintao and Gao, Haitao and Ruan, Mixue and Guo, Hai and Zhou, Xiaojie and Zhang, Di",
    title = "Design of vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on GA-Fuzzy-PID algorithm",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "PLOS ONE",
    volume = "18",
    pages = "e0293823",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.1371/journal.pone.0293823",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0293823",
    publisher = "Public Library of Science (PLoS)",
    issue = "11",
    issn = "1932-6203"
}


Abstract:
As is well known, the metal annealing process has the characteristics of heat concentration and rapid heating. Traditional vacuum annealing furnaces use PID control method, which has problems such as high temperature fluctuation, large overshoot, and long response time during the heating and heating process. Based on this situation, some domestic scholars have adopted fuzzy PID control algorithm in the temperature control of vacuum annealing furnaces. Due to the fact that fuzzy rules are formulated through a large amount of on-site temperature data and experience summary, there is a certain degree of subjectivity, which cannot ensure that each rule is optimal. In response to this drawback, the author combined the technical parameters of vacuum annealing furnace equipment, The fuzzy PID temperature control of the vacuum annealing furnace is optimized using genetic algorithm. Through simulation and comparative analysis, it is concluded that the design of the fuzzy PID vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on GA optimization is superior to fuzzy PID and traditional PID control in terms of temperature accuracy, rise time, and overshoot control. Finally, it was verified through offline experiments that the fuzzy PID temperature control system based on GA optimization meets the annealing temperature requirements of metal workpieces and can be applied to the temperature control system of vacuum annealing furnaces.

Abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Downstream processing of steel products involves a large variety of heat treatments in the ferrite as well as in the austenite phase. Different from austenite conditioning in the upstream processing stage, deformation and temperature schedules are usually decoupled in downstream processing. Fundamental characteristics of microalloying elements regarding their effects on recovery, recrystallization, phase transformation, and grain growth during downstream processing will be reviewed. Relevant characteristics of microalloying elements are the atom size, the solubility and temperature stability of related carbides or nitrides, the tendency of segregating at grain boundaries, dislocations and point defects, as well as the diffusivity. The various interactions and resulting microstructural effects will be demonstrated for several industrial steel developments. The selected examples cover microalloy effects in ferrite and austenite phases, under slow and fast thermal cycles, as well as during short and long heat treatment times. Based on the observations and the related interpretation, a qualitative understanding of the microalloy interactions will be developed that is generally applicable despite the considerable variation of processes and products. It will also be highlighted how appropriate process and microalloy adaptations resulted in remarkable product optimization under industrial conditions.</jats:p>

## 3. Study of Grain Growth in a Ni-Based Superalloy by Experiments and Cellular Automaton Model
Valid Text Names:
'liu2021studyofgrain pages 1-2', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 2-5', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 5-6', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 6-8', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 8-10', 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 10-11'

BibTex:
@article{liu2021studyofgrain,
    author = "Liu, Yan-Xing and Ke, Zhi-Jiang and Li, Run-Hua and Song, Ju-Qing and Ruan, Jing-Jing",
    title = "Study of Grain Growth in a Ni-Based Superalloy by Experiments and Cellular Automaton Model",
    year = "2021",
    journal = "Materials",
    volume = "14",
    pages = "6922",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.3390/ma14226922",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/ma14226922",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "22",
    issn = "1996-1944"
}


Abstract:
The grain growth behavior in a typical Ni-based superalloy was investigated using isothermal heat treatment experiments over a holding temperature range of 1353–1473 K. The experimental results showed that the grain structure continuously coarsened as the holding time and holding temperature increased during heat treatment. A classical parabolic grain growth model was used to explore the mechanism of grain growth under experimental conditions. The grain growth exponent was found to be slightly above 2. This indicates that the current grain growth in the studied superalloy is mainly governed by grain boundary migration with a minor pinning effect from the precipitates. Then, the grain growth in the studied superalloy during isothermal heat treatment was modelled by a cellular automaton (CA) with deterministic state switch rules. The microscale kinetics of grain growth is described by the correlation between the moving velocity and curvature of the grain boundary. The local grain boundary curvature is well evaluated by a template disk method. The grain boundary mobility was found to increase with increasing temperature. The relationship between the grain boundary mobility and temperature has been established. The developed CA model is capable of capturing the dependence of the grain size on the holding time under different holding temperatures.

Abstract:
<jats:p>The wear behaviour of AISI 431stainless steel was investigated at consistent load under a dry sliding touch with vacuum annealing device. The approach implemented was found to have been effective in improving the wear actions of stainless steel products, advancing the pliability properties. For Vacuum Annealed (VA1) 2 hours, Vacuum Annealed (VA2) 4 hours and Vacuum Annealed (VA3) 6 hours respectively, a low temperature heat treatment was performed on sample for the maximum annealing cycle. Untreated stainless steel (UNT) was used as a kind of viewpoint tool for contrast with specific hardening samples and conducted wear checks. The hardening samples show certain hard formed layers which enhance the hardness of the surface. Estimations of small scale toughness showed a crucial improvement in toughness following operation. This method raises the hardness of the substance up to 150 percent. A pin on the disc drive is used to do wear checking to assess wear failure. The layers were represented by optical lens magnification and the examination of electron magnifying glass.</jats:p>

    title = "Kinetic Analysis of the Austenite Grain Growth in GCr15 Steel",
    year = "2010",
    journal = "Journal of Materials Engineering and Performance",
    volume = "19",
    pages = "112-115",
    month = "Feb",
    doi = "10.1007/s11665-009-9413-y",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1007/s11665-009-9413-y",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "1059-9495"
}


Abstract:
The growth behavior of austenite grains in GCr15 steel was investigated through the isothermal annealing tests of the steel under different heating temperatures and holding times. The tests were performed on a Gleeble-3800 thermo-mechanical simulation machine. Austenitizing temperatures 1223, 1323, 1373, and 1423 K were chosen, and holding time varied from 0 to 480 s. Experimental results suggest that austenite grains grow gradually with the increase of heating temperature, and holding time has an important effect on the growth of austenite grains. The time exponent for the growth is bigger at higher temperature, and the growth rate decreases with increasing time. On the basis of previous models and experimental results, a mathematical model that can describe the growth behavior of austenite grains in the tested steel under different heating temperatures and holding times was obtained using regression analysis. The predicted grain sizes by the model are in good agreement with measured ones.

## 6. Microstructural evolution, recovery and recrystallization kinetics of isothermally annealed ultra low carbon steel
Valid Text Names:
'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 1-4', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 4-6', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 6-11', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 11-14', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 14-17', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 17-20', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 20-22', 'dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery pages 22-25'

BibTex:
@article{dutta2020microstructuralevolutionrecovery,
    author = "Dutta, Siuli and Panda, Ashis K and Mitra, Amitava and Chatterjee, Subrata and Roy, Rajat K",
    title = "Microstructural evolution, recovery and recrystallization kinetics of isothermally annealed ultra low carbon steel",
    year = "2020",
    journal = "Materials Research Express",
    volume = "7",
    pages = "016554",
    month = "Jan",
    doi = "10.1088/2053-1591/ab657c",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1088/2053-1591/ab657c",
    publisher = "IOP Publishing",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "2053-1591"
}


Abstract:
The recovery and recrystallization kinetics of 80% cold rolled ultra low carbon steel are investigated during isothermally annealing for temperature ranges 350–640 °C as a function of different annealing time. The recovery is assessed by magnetic coercivity (Hc), while the recrystallization is determined by mechanical hardness. At low temperature (350 to 520 °C) annealing, recovery dominates for long time (∼12 000 s), while the annealing at 550 °C/ 900s and 580 °C/ 300s causes the recrystallized nuclei formation . The recovery kinetics is introduced by differential rate equation, explaining the reduction in coercivity with the recovery progress and the variation of an activation energy from 41–113 kJ mol−1. The recrystallization kinetics is found faster at high annealing temperature 640 °C than 550 and 580 °C based on hardness measurement, justifying by apparent activation energy within 114–190 kJ mol−1. Furthermore, the recovery and recrystallization rate increase with different annealing time, consistent to the change of microstructures and grain boundary characteristics evaluated by the orientation imaging microscopy (OIM) of electron backscattered diffraction (EBSD).

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'hirota2008recrystallizationandgrain pages 6-7'.

Noriaki Hirota, Fuxing Yin, Tadanobu Inoue, and Tsukasa Azuma. Recrystallization and grain growth behavior in severe cold-rolling deformed sus316l steel under anisothermal annealing condition. Isij International, 48:475-482, Apr 2008. URL: https://doi.org/10.2355/isijinternational.48.475, doi:10.2355/isijinternational.48.475. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports anisothermal annealing experiments on cold-rolled SUS316L between 1 073 and 1 223 K with heating rates 0.031–9.3 K/s. It shows that the critical recrystallization finish temperature (Ti) increases approximately linearly with heating rate and that heating rate effects on grain growth are stronger when modeled using an empirical exp(0.5/q) factor rather than 1/q. High heating rates produce many dispersed recrystallization nucleation sites and yield an ideal lognormal grain-size distribution and ultrafine annealed microstructures; low heating rates allow longer dwell at elevated temperature, nucleation at preferred deformation features, and selective grain growth of some recrystallized grains. The kinetic model used is valid only for a strictly linear heating profile, and a more accurate treatment is needed when heating-rate nonlinearity exists. Controlling grain size therefore requires (a) choice of annealing temperature and heating rate (higher rates favor finer grains and more nucleation), (b) control of time-at-temperature (slow heating increases dwell time and grain growth), and (c) a homogeneous deformation microstructure (e.g., multipass rolling or grain-boundary engineering) to promote simultaneous nucleation and ultrafine final grain size. The paper also fits grain-growth parameters (K, n) and notes they correlate with isothermal values when appropriately adjusted for heating rate.
