## pqac-00000001

From text 'digges1966heattreatmentand pages 13-15'.

Thomas G Digges and Samuel J Rosenberg. Heat treatment and properties of iron and steel. ArXiv, Oct 1966. URL: https://doi.org/10.6028/nbs.mono.18, doi:10.6028/nbs.mono.18. This article has 94 citations.

The excerpt gives general annealing guidance relevant to vacuum annealing for stress relief and homogenization though it does not discuss vacuum specifically. For full annealing, steels are heated above the transformation range (Acs) and typically held at about 25–50°F above the upper-critical line. Process (stress-relief) annealing is performed close to but below Aci; if no further cold work is intended, a lower relief range of about 1,000°F is often sufficient. Examples in the text show process annealing at ~1,200°F and full annealing at ~1,650°F. A commonly cited soak-time rule is "% hr/in. of thickness" (soak time scales with section thickness). The goal of soaking is to secure uniform temperature throughout the part; thicker sections therefore govern required soak time. Spheroidizing is achieved by prolonged heating just below Aci (final hold just below Ari), typically near ~1,000°F for high-carbon steels. Grain growth is influenced by temperature and time (full annealing produces larger ferrite grains than process annealing), so control is achieved by limiting soak temperature/time, using lower-temperature stress-relief cycles where possible, ensuring uniform heating (preheating/transfers to reduce local overheating), and matching soak to section thickness.

## pqac-00000002

From text 'hirota2008recrystallizationandgrain pages 1-2'.

Noriaki Hirota, Fuxing Yin, Tadanobu Inoue, and Tsukasa Azuma. Recrystallization and grain growth behavior in severe cold-rolling deformed sus316l steel under anisothermal annealing condition. Isij International, 48:475-482, Apr 2008. URL: https://doi.org/10.2355/isijinternational.48.475, doi:10.2355/isijinternational.48.475. This article has 19 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This study on SUS316L austenitic stainless steel examines anisothermal annealing (1073–1223 K) with heating rates from 0.031 to 9.3 K/s and links heating rate and temperature to recrystallization and grain growth. Recrystallization finish temperature was identified by a hardness criterion and found to vary linearly with the logarithm of heating rate. At the defined critical temperature the average grain size was ≈2.2 mm at low heating rates, but grain size decreased rapidly for heating rates above ~1 K/s. A grain-growth kinetics expression for anisothermal annealing was applied; the proportional term P was found to follow P = K·exp(0.5/q), and the grain growth exponent n was determined between 2.5 and 3.0. EBSD showed that low heating rates produced partial recrystallization with preferred orientations at completion, whereas high heating rates (>1 K/s) produced homogeneously nucleated, randomly oriented grains with a lognormal size distribution. The paper therefore indicates that fast heating (higher q) and control of peak temperature/time strongly influence final grain size and microstructure during annealing.

## 1. Precipitation and grain growth modelling in Ti-Nb microalloyed steels

    author = "Graux, Alexis and Cazottes, Sophie and Castro, David De and Martín, David San and Capdevila, Carlos and Cabrera, Jose Maria and Molas, Sílvia and Schreiber, Sebastian and Mirković, Djordje and Danoix, Frédéric and Bugnet, Matthieu and Fabrègue, Damien and Perez, Michel",

    month = "Mar",

    url = "https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtla.2019.100233",
    publisher = "Elsevier BV",
    issn = "2589-1529"
}


Abstract:
Abstract Mechanical properties of microalloyed steels are enhanced by fine precipitates, that ensure grain growth control during subsequent heat treatment. This study aims at predicting austenite grain growth kinetics coupling a precipitation model and a grain growth model. These models were applied to a titanium and niobium microalloyed steel. The precipitate size distributions were first characterized by TEM and SEM and prior austenite grain boundaries were revealed by thermal etching after various isothermal treatments. From CALPHAD database, a solubility product was determined for (Ti,Nb)C precipitates. A numerical model based on the classical nucleation and growth theories was used to predict the time evolution of (Ti,Nb)C size distributions during various isothermal heat treatments. The precipitation model was validated from TEM/SEM analysis. The resulting precipitate size distributions served as entry parameters to a simple grain growth model based on Zener pinning. The pinning pressure was calculated using the whole size distribution. The resulting austenite grain growth kinetics were in good agreement with the experimental data obtained for all investigated heat treatments.

Abstract:
The steel industry is interested in developing high strength steels for lightweight applications. Typically, austenitic stainless steels have good ductility and formability but their yield strength is quite low.1) There are several methods to improve the strength, e.g., solid solute strengthening, work hardening and grain size refinement.2) Many austenitic stainless steels are metastable at the room temperature so that during cold working some strain-induced martensite (SIM) forms, thereby increasing their strength3) but reducing ductility.2) Therefore, other strengthening methods such as grain size refinement are preferred.4–8) An ultrafine grain size can be obtained by heavy cold deformation to create a high fraction of SIM that during subsequent proper annealing treatment reverts back to austenite. Martensitic reversion has already been investigated and reported during the 1970’s, as referred by Tomimura et al.4) They also noted the formation of a fine austenite grain size obtained from reversed martensite in their experimental Cr–Ni steels. Since then, grain refinement by the martensitic reversion treatment in both Cr–Ni and Cr–Mn austenitic stainless steels has been considered in numerous papers, Effect of Nb Microalloying on Reversion and Grain Growth in a High-Mn 204Cu Austenitic Stainless Steel

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000003

From text 'cheruvathur2016additivemanufacturingof pages 7-11'.

Sudha Cheruvathur, Eric A. Lass, and Carelyn E. Campbell. Additive manufacturing of 17-4 ph stainless steel: post-processing heat treatment to achieve uniform reproducible microstructure. JOM, 68:930-942, Dec 2016. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11837-015-1754-4, doi:10.1007/s11837-015-1754-4. This article has 378 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt documents heat-treatment behavior of additively manufactured 17-4 PH stainless steel relevant to stress relief and homogenization. For cast/alloy analogs, AMS 5355 recommends a homogenization heat treatment at 1150 C for >90 min followed by solutionizing at 1050 C to reduce segregation and approach a more uniform microstructure. The MC carbide solvus lies above ~1250 C, so treatments below this (including 1050 C solutionizing) will not fully dissolve MC carbides, explaining observed carbide coarsening after solutionizing. At 1050 C the equilibrium is FCC-Austenite + MC niobium carbides. Stress-relieving (EOS) treatments increased hardness (reported 312 ± 17 VHN) while still retaining some FCC/austenite; more aggressive anneals (AM-S) produced predominantly martensite and reduced retained austenite to ~15–20% but niobium carbides still coarsened, indicating anneal times/temperatures were insufficient to fully homogenize microchemistry. The text also notes microsegregation is present and that multiple tempering cycles cause "coarsening of carbides," while cryogenic treatments (dry ice) can modify secondary carbide populations. These points indicate temperature thresholds and limits for carbide dissolution and homogenization, and highlight trade-offs between hardness, retained phases, and carbide coarsening.


---

## pqac-00000004

From text 'tian2009experimentalandsimulation pages 6-7'.

Gaofeng Tian, Chengchang Jia, Jiantao Liu, and Benfu Hu. Experimental and simulation on the grain growth of p/m nickel-base superalloy during the heat treatment process. Materials & Design, 30:433-439, Mar 2009. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007, doi:10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007. This article has 71 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt reports a simulation and experimental study of gradient heat treatment of a P/M nickel-base superalloy disk. The authors used a furnace temperature 20–40°C above the c0 solvus (1150°C in this study). Temperature was monitored at six radial points; with increasing hold time the radial temperature gradient reduced and temperatures tended toward homogeneity. At a 60 min hold the inner points A–C were below 1110°C while outer points D–F were 1110–1145°C, implying inner regions grow more slowly and outer regions can grow rapidly. To achieve a dual microstructure the authors recommend holding time should be less than 60 min. They note a treatment window where bore <1110°C and rim 1130–1150°C yields fine/coarse region grain grades (bore ~9–10, rim ~5.5–6). Heat transfer in their model included convection and radiation between disk, furnace wall and furnace air. Monte Carlo simulation was used to model grain growth. These points inform soak temperature selection relative to solvus, the importance of radial temperature control and limiting hold time to reduce undesirable grain growth during homogenization/stress-relief.


---

## pqac-00000005

From text 'mohrbacher2026applicationofmicroalloying pages 4-6'.

Hardy Mohrbacher. Application of microalloying for controlling recrystallization and grain growth during downstream steel processing. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:2548-2571, Mar 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5, doi:10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt provides annealing guidance relevant to controlling recovery, recrystallization and grain growth in steels that can inform vacuum annealing schedules. It emphasizes using the Hollomon–Jaffe (HP) parameter to combine temperature and time into a single metric (HP = T·[log(t)+20]·10^-3 as presented) and reports empirical HP ranges: recovery for HP ≈ 16–19.3, partial microstructures in the intermediate range, and full recrystallization and grain growth when HP > 20.5. Microalloying (especially solute niobium and molybdenum) strongly retards recovery/recrystallization and widens the stable recovery window, while precipitation (e.g., 50–60 pct of available Nb) or raising peak temperatures to promote precipitation reduces solute drag and can permit grain growth. Practical observations include that 0.04 wt% Nb steel required ~80× longer at 700 °C to reach 95% recrystallization, and that in continuous lines short cycle times (≈120 s) may force peak temperatures toward ~800 °C. Lower coiling/soak temperatures retain microalloy in solution and give finer grains; the achievable recrystallized grain size limit is ≈3 µm. Overall, select soak T–t to keep HP below recrystallization thresholds, coordinate with alloy solute/precipitation behavior, and use microalloying/precipitation control to prevent unwanted grain growth.


---

## pqac-00000006

From text 'seo2020effectofmo pages 1-2'.

Eun Jung Seo, John G. Speer, David K. Matlock, and Robert L. Cryderman. Effect of mo in combination with nb on austenite grain size control in vacuum carburizing steels. Apr 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11665-020-04751-8, doi:10.1007/s11665-020-04751-8. This article has 24 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt discusses vacuum carburizing at elevated temperatures (up to ~1100°C) to shorten cycle times and increase productivity, but it warns that higher carburizing temperatures promote austenite grain growth, which can degrade fatigue performance. To limit austenite grain coarsening during carburizing, microalloying to form small carbo-nitride precipitates is effective. Nb additions alone provide some control of abnormal austenite grain growth, while Mo combined with Nb gives enhanced resistance to grain growth, particularly at high carburizing temperatures up to ~1050°C; this enhanced control is attributed to solute and precipitation effects. The paper also describes two pre-heat treatment approaches: (1) austenitizing with isothermal annealing to alter ferrite/pearlite morphology and (2) a subcritical anneal to soften by annealing ferrite and bainite. The excerpt contrasts conventional gas carburizing temperatures (920–940°C) with vacuum carburizing and notes vacuum processing advantages (reduced intergranular oxidation, cleaner surfaces, compressed gas quenching) and that compressed gas quenching may require higher hardenability. These points are relevant to strategies for avoiding grain growth during high-temperature vacuum furnace treatments.


---

## pqac-00000007

From text 'mohrbacher2026applicationofmicroalloying pages 1-2'.

Hardy Mohrbacher. Application of microalloying for controlling recrystallization and grain growth during downstream steel processing. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:2548-2571, Mar 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5, doi:10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt reviews how microalloying (notably niobium, titanium, vanadium, molybdenum) influences recovery, recrystallization, phase transformation, and grain growth during downstream thermal processing. Key points relevant to annealing and grain-growth control: thermal cycle durations used downstream span seconds to tens of hours and heating/cooling rates can be extremely fast or very slow, so soak times and rates strongly affect microalloy state. Precipitation in hot austenite is relatively slow and follows a C‑curve with a nose around 900–930 °C, meaning time/temperature windows exist where precipitate nucleation/growth is most rapid. Ti and Nb are strong carbide/nitride formers with low solubility, and several microalloy atoms are larger than iron, promoting segregation to boundaries/dislocations; these attributes (plus solute drag and precipitate pinning quantified by the SRP/TNR concepts) retard recrystallization and grain growth. Deformation accelerates particle nucleation, altering precipitate evolution during subsequent anneals. These mechanistic observations imply that controlling soak temperature relative to precipitation kinetics, tailoring heating/cooling rates, and leveraging microalloy chemistry/precipitates are central strategies to achieve stress relief/homogenization while preventing excessive grain growth.


---

## pqac-00000008

From text 'mohrbacher2026applicationofmicroalloying pages 2-4'.

Hardy Mohrbacher. Application of microalloying for controlling recrystallization and grain growth during downstream steel processing. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:2548-2571, Mar 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5, doi:10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt discusses how annealing temperature and time control precipitation, solute drag, and grain-boundary pinning in microalloyed steels, which is directly relevant to stress-relief/homogenization and grain-growth control. It notes that precipitation kinetics accelerate at higher annealing temperatures—"completeness of precipitation is achieved in a shorter time period at higher annealing temperature"—and that particle size grows with longer isothermal holds, especially "when annealing above the Curie temperature due to the significantly better diffusivity of niobium in paramagnetic ferrite." Smaller second-phase particles are more effective at Zener pinning ("For the same second-phase volume fraction, smaller particle sizes are more effective in pinning grain-boundary motion"), while elongated particles can be particularly effective at retarding grain growth ("second-phase particles with an elongated morphology are more effective in retarding grain growth"). For austenite, microalloy precipitation is slow and follows a C-curve with a nose near 900–930 C, so precipitates often do not reach completeness during industrial austenite conditioning. The text also emphasizes the role of solute drag ("solute drag has a relatively more significant contribution to austenite conditioning") and that the overall "time–temperature schedule ... determines the microstructural features." These points inform decisions on soak temperature/time trade-offs: higher temperature shortens required soak for precipitation and homogenization but promotes coarsening/grain growth unless pinned by fine or elongated second-phase particles or by solute drag; alloy content and process (batch vs continuous) strongly affect outcomes.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000009

From text 'digges1966heattreatmentand pages 36-37'.

Thomas G Digges and Samuel J Rosenberg. Heat treatment and properties of iron and steel. ArXiv, Oct 1966. URL: https://doi.org/10.6028/nbs.mono.18, doi:10.6028/nbs.mono.18. This article has 94 citations.

The provided excerpt contains Table 6, a catalog of recommended heat treatments for many AISI/SAE steels, including a dedicated "Annealing temperature" column with temperature ranges given in °F for individual grades (examples: 1040: 1,476–1,526°F; 4140: 1,500–1,550°F; 4340: 1,600–1,650°F). The table is oriented to heat-treating grades and pairs normalizing, annealing, hardening temperatures and suggested quenching media. Accompanying notes state that omitted steels can be estimated by interpolation and that the upper end of the heat-treating ranges should be used for large sections. The excerpt does not explicitly label anneals as "stress-relief" versus "homogenization," nor does it provide soak times per inch of thickness or specific guidance for stainless steels or superalloys. Quench-media guidance and section-size considerations are present, but explicit soak-time rules and annealing classifications (stress relief/homogenization) for tool steels, carbon steels, stainless steels, and superalloys are not included in this excerpt.

  In this 100th anniversary year of ASM International, it is especially ﬁtting to release ASM Handbook,
Volume 4A, Steel Heat Treating Fundamentals and Processes. Since its 1913 origin as the Steel Treaters
Club, formed by Detroit blacksmith William Park Woodside, ASM International has grown in scope; yet
steel heat treating remains a core subject of the Society. Woodside’s vision and recognition of the need to
exchange information on steel heat treating are further recognized by many successful publications
including the renowned Metals Handbook.

  The ASM Handbook (formerly Metals Handbook) series is being expanded into several volumes on
heat treatment. This reﬂects the roots of ASM International, as well as the Heat Treating Society (An
Afﬁliate Society of ASM International) with its ongoing member contributions in the ﬁeld of heat treat-
ing. ASM International and the Heat Treating Society extend a very special thanks to George E. Totten
and Jon Dossett as Volume Editors. Their initiatives and contributions were instrumental in the develop-
ment of this Volume. We are indebted to them and to the subject editors, authors, and reviewers for this
publication.

Abstract:
In the present work, we introduce a state parameter-based microstructure evolution model, which incorporates the effect of solute atoms and precipitates on recrystallization kinetics. The model accounts for local precipitate coarsening at grain boundaries, which promotes an average grain boundary movement even if the Zener pinning force exceeds the driving force for recrystallization. The impact of solute drag on the grain boundary mobility as well as simultaneous precipitation is discussed in detail. The model is validated on experimental data on recrystallization in V-micro-alloyed steel, where excellent agreement is achieved.

## 7. Mean Field Modeling of Grain Growth and Zener Pinning

Abstract:
A mean-field model has been developed to simulate curvature-driven grain growth by exploring the evolution of grain size distribution under arbitrary thermal histories. The model was integrated into precipitation module TC-PRISMA, so that the pinning effect of the concurrently precipitated particles on the growing grains can be considered by a modified, location-specific Zener model. The developed model was validated against analytical calculations, and then applied to real alloy systems, fed with assessed grain boundary energy and mobility data. Its capabilities, limitations, and directions to improvements have been discussed.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000013

From text 'kisko2015effectofnb pages 6-7'.

Anna Kisko, Juho Talonen, David Arthur Porter, and Leo Pentti Karjalainen. Effect of nb microalloying on reversion and grain growth in a high-mn 204cu austenitic stainless steel. Isij International, 55:2217-2224, Oct 2015. URL: https://doi.org/10.2355/isijinternational.isijint-2015-156, doi:10.2355/isijinternational.isijint-2015-156. This article has 29 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt discusses Zener-type pinning of austenite grain boundaries by Nb-containing precipitates and the roles of particle size (r) and volume fraction (f). Only particles <50 nm were used for pinning-force calculations because these fine precipitates are effective at boundary pinning. Theoretical calculations assume all Nb is in precipitates (NbC or NbN). Driving forces (Fd) were evaluated using Zener and Gladman expressions (Fd = 2γ/D form) and pinning forces (Fp) using Zener-based models in rigid boundary (RBM) and flexible boundary (FBM) forms; the equations show Fp scales with f, γ and inversely with r (different prefactors for Eqs. (5)–(7)). Parameters used include γ = 0.8 J/m2 and size advantage Z = 2. TEM-measured mean radii (Table 3) and volume fractions (Table 4) were used (e.g. for 0.28Nb f ≈ 0.29/0.26/0.308; for 0.45Nb f ≈ 0.50/0.50/0.495). Comparisons of Fd and Fp indicate that higher Nb (≈0.28 wt.% and above) yields sufficient pinning (especially in FBM) to prevent grain coarsening at 1373 K, while lower Nb steels show some grain growth. Equating Fd and Fp implies a limiting grain size that increases with particle radius and decreases with increasing particle volume fraction (D_lim roughly ∝ r/f).


---

## pqac-00000014

From text 'sha2009graingrowthbehavior pages 3-6'.

Qingyun Sha and Zuqing Sun. Grain growth behavior of coarse-grained austenite in a nb-v-ti microalloyed steel. Materials Science and Engineering A-structural Materials Properties Microstructure and Processing, 523:77-84, Oct 2009. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msea.2009.05.037, doi:10.1016/j.msea.2009.05.037. This article has 107 citations.

The authors evaluate Zener-type pinning of austenite grain boundaries by precipitates using several models (Zener, rigid boundary model (RBM), flexible boundary model (FBM)) and driving-force expressions (Zener and Gladman). Assumptions used include Gladman Z = 2 and an interfacial energy of 0.8 J/m2; a maximum grain size of 1600 µm was used for driving-force calculations. Because Ti-rich carbonitrides are rectangular rather than spherical, an equivalent radius was computed to use spherical-particle expressions, and precipitate volume fraction was estimated by thermodynamic calculation. Calculations show FBM predicts larger pinning forces than Zener or RBM (more particles interacting with boundaries). Comparing pinning (Fp) and driving (Fd) forces indicates driving forces exceed pinning only for smaller grains (<600 µm); for larger grains pinning forces are larger (values close to each other), implying an equilibrium between grain coarsening and precipitate coarsening. The strong pinning by Ti-rich carbonitrides below 1250 ◦C inhibits grain growth (fine and coarse austenite) and abnormal grain growth would be expected only at higher temperatures.


---

## pqac-00000015

From text 'ringer1990oninteractionsand pages 77-83'.

On interactions and reactions between migrating grain and phase boundaries, and precipitate particles This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt discusses Zener-type pinning of austenite grain boundaries by second-phase particles, emphasizing that limiting grain size during annealing is controlled by particle volume fraction (f) and mean particle size (R). Maximum grain refinement is achieved with a high volume fraction and the smallest possible mean particle size; particles formed at lower temperatures (solid-state precipitation) are most effective, while those formed during solidification tend to be coarser and less effective. Simulations and analyses reported show a stronger inhibition of grain growth than classic Zener/Smith random-interaction predictions, with the fraction of particle–boundary interactions and the dependence of pinned grain size on f being larger than 2D expectations. TiN in microalloyed steels is given as a case study: TiN precipitates as fine, stable cuboids with low solubility, making it particularly effective at pinning and resistant to coarsening. Particle coarsening during thermal exposure follows the Lifshitz–Wagner kinetics, so lower dissolved solute (e.g., [Ti]) and faster cooling reduce coarsening and improve pinning efficacy. Overall, smaller R, larger f, low solute levels and minimized coarsening give the smallest limiting grain size.


---

## pqac-00000016

From text 'wu2022meanfieldmodeling pages 6-7'.

Kaisheng Wu, Johan Jeppsson, and Paul Mason. Mean field modeling of grain growth and zener pinning. Journal of Phase Equilibria and Diffusion, 43:866-875, Nov 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11669-022-01005-z, doi:10.1007/s11669-022-01005-z. This article has 15 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt discusses numerical mean-field modeling of particle pinning (Zener pinning) and its effect on limiting grain size during annealing. Zener parameters (K and m) from Hillert (K = 0.222, m = 0.93) were used to compute a Zener radius (about 8 lm), but numerical results indicate adjustments are needed because the simulated ratio of Rm to RCr (~0.5–0.6) is lower than the 0.75 assumed in prior theory. For the special case Rm = 0.5 RCr, the model predicts RCr = Rz (the Zener radius). Simulations show strong dependence on initial grain size: small initial grains (0.2 lm) produce discontinuous, stepwise growth with multiple plateaus as precipitates nucleate/coarsen and pin grain boundaries; larger initial grains (3.2 lm) show continuous but slowed growth and deviation from parabolic kinetics. The model uses an Arrhenius grain-boundary mobility (M = M0 exp(-Q/RT)) and a calibrated mobility giving ~7.94e-16 m4/Js at 722 °C. Overall, particle size, volume fraction (via Zener radius), and initial grain size control whether growth is arrested near the Zener radius or continues with reduced kinetics.


---

## pqac-00000017

From text 'ringer1990oninteractionsand pages 167-174'.

On interactions and reactions between migrating grain and phase boundaries, and precipitate particles This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt gives a Zener-type relationship for the limiting grain size D set by precipitate pinning: D = (1/K) · d / f (equation 9.9), i.e. limiting grain size scales directly with particle size d and inversely with particle volume fraction f, with K an efficiency factor. K depends on particle shape, crystallographic orientation and particle-matrix coherency: cubic particles generally exert larger drag than spheres, and coherent precipitates provide the highest resistance to boundary migration. For spheres a form used is K = 3.(1 + cos y0)/2. The pressures in Table 9.1 are maximum values based on assumptions (all particles similarly oriented) and an approximation for surface density; orientation distributions should be averaged if present. The authors note the coherency degree alters pinning (plots shown in figures 9.5–9.6) and that the quantitative equations could be improved with better statistical treatment, but the approximations give reasonable order-of-magnitude results. Measurements discussed concern Ti-microalloyed steel prior-austenite grains.


---

## pqac-00000018

From text 'ringer1990oninteractionsand pages 90-95'.

On interactions and reactions between migrating grain and phase boundaries, and precipitate particles This article has 0 citations.

The excerpt discusses Zener (Zener/Smith) pinning of austenite grain boundaries by TiN precipitates and emphasizes that both particle volume fraction and particle size strongly control limiting grain size during annealing. Experimental data (Pickering) show clear effects of TiN volume fraction and particle size on austenite grain size. Maintaining small, coarsening-resistant TiN particles requires low dissolved Ti in austenite and thus relatively high dissolved N at a given austenitising temperature; this condition is favored by a hypostoichiometric composition. Rapid solidification (e.g., continuous casting) refines TiN formed during solidification, permitting more hypostoichiometry. However, predictability of limiting grain size using a simple Zener/Smith relation is limited because particle shape (non-spherical), coarsening behavior, interactions with other microalloying elements (V, Nb), and solute levels complicate the microstructure. Related models for recrystallization (Cotterill) show that reducing interparticle spacing transitions behavior from accelerated to severely retarded recrystallization, indicating that pinning pressure and kinetics depend on spacing, size, and particle distribution.


---

## pqac-00000019

From text 'ringer1989ontheinteraction pages 5-7'.

S.P. Ringer, W.B. Li, and K.E. Easterling. On the interaction and pinning of grain boundaries by cubic shaped precipitate particles. Acta Metallurgica, 37:831-841, Mar 1989. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/0001-6160(89)90010-2, doi:10.1016/0001-6160(89)90010-2. This article has 142 citations.

Pages 5–7 discuss how cubic precipitates interact with migrating grain boundaries and how that interaction affects Zener-type pinning. The total system energy (particle plus boundary) depends on particle orientation; as a boundary moves it changes grain boundary area and the inclusion–matrix interface energy (coherent to incoherent), producing a rotation moment (force) on cubic particles. The paper gives a general relationship for that moment (for changes in the particle orientation angle) and notes the maximum moments are typically positive, so boundaries tend to rotate particles to increase the orientation angle. The magnitude of the moment depends on interaction angles, interfacial energy change, and coherency. Experimentally, the authors tested new Zener pinning equations using Ti-microalloyed and 321 austenitic steels with controlled isothermal and short anneals; particle size, volume fraction, and prior austenitic grain size were measured. Volume fractions were measured from thin-foil TEM techniques (known foil thickness, fringe/extinction methods) and extraction replicas, mean particle size from ~1000-particle statistics, and grain size by optical microscopy with ASTM linear intercept and planimetric analysis. These measurements were used to evaluate limiting grain size vs particle size/volume fraction in annealing.


---

## pqac-00000020

From text 'mohrbacher2026applicationofmicroalloying pages 2-4'.

Hardy Mohrbacher. Application of microalloying for controlling recrystallization and grain growth during downstream steel processing. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:2548-2571, Mar 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5, doi:10.1007/s11661-026-08172-5. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt highlights factors relevant to Zener pinning during annealing. It states that for a given second-phase volume fraction, smaller precipitate sizes are more effective at pinning grain-boundary motion, and that elongated particle morphologies provide stronger retardation of grain growth. Coherent particle/matrix interfaces can increase pinning potency through interfacial and misfit-generated elastic energies. Precipitate size grows with isothermal hold time and more rapidly at higher annealing temperatures (notably above the Curie point for ferrite), while precipitation in austenite is relatively slow (C-curve with a nose around 900–930 °C) and can be substantially suppressed depending on coiling/annealing strategy. NbC particles typically show an aspect ratio ~2–2.5 and may change morphology (e.g., to more spherical and incoherent upon transformation), which also affects their pinning effectiveness. These observations collectively bear on how particle size, volume fraction, morphology, interface coherence, and processing thermal history control the limiting grain size via Zener-type pinning during annealing.

                    <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                    grain evolution take place quickly during the heating step to the solution temperature and is most likely governed by static recrystallization, where the final grain size distribution is defined by the density of recrystallized grain nuclei whose boundaries migrate to consume the residual work hardened grains in the microstructure. Microstructure with limited work hardening (

                    determined as having a 0.16 and 0.38 deg grain-surface averaged grain averaged misorientation and grain orientation spread respectively) can form heterogenous grain structures composed of some grains of much larger size than their neighbors (> 200

                    m), which decreases the material’s fatigue resistance. Once the residual work hardening is consumed, the grain structure shortly remains stable due to the pinning pressure exerted by nanometric (Hf, Zr)O
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    oxide and (Nb, Ti)C carbide particles on grain boundaries. On slightly work-hardened sample material, a slower heating rate to the solution temperature increases the propensity to form grains of excessive size (> 200 
                    <jats:italic>µ</jats:italic>
                    m) and rather heterogeneous grain size distribution. Additional interrupted SHTs, microstructural investigations and rough calculations of the driving pressures exerted on the grain boundaries illustrated that such a phenomenon could be favored by several concomitant factors during the 2 °C/min temperature ramp up to the SHT temperature, such as ripening of the
                    <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                    ′ precipitates population during their dissolution, limited statically recrystallized nuclei and exposure to a slightly positive grain boundary driving pressure balance over a prolonged period of time.
                  </jats:p>

## 3. Effect of heat treatment and hot working on microstructure and mechanical properties of a novel nickel base superalloy
Valid Text Names:
'shakhov2018effectofheat pages 1-2', 'shakhov2018effectofheat pages 2-5', 'shakhov2018effectofheat pages 5-5'

BibTex:
@article{shakhov2018effectofheat,
    author = "Shakhov, R. V. and Ganeev, A. A. and Mukhtarov, Sh. Kh. and Logunov, A. V.",
    title = "Effect of heat treatment and hot working on microstructure and mechanical properties of a novel nickel base superalloy",
    year = "2018",
    journal = "Letters on Materials",
    volume = "8",
    pages = "494-498",
    month = "Dec",
    doi = "10.22226/2410-3535-2018-4-494-498",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.22226/2410-3535-2018-4-494-498",
    publisher = "Institute for Metals Superplasticity Problems of RAS",
    issue = "4",
    issn = "2218-5046"
}


Abstract:
The microstructure and mechanical properties of a novel nickel base superalloy intended for manufacturing of die tools and parts for gas turbine engine have been investigated. The superalloy designated as SLZhS-1R had the following composition: Ni-47(Al,Cr,Co,Ta,W,Hf)-0.2(C,B) (wt. %). The as-cast superalloy was subjected to homogenization and heterogenization heat treatment, which is typically performed before hot working. However, the heat treatment led to appearance of platelike precipitates, which are not favorable for the hot workability. Therefore, the superalloy was subjected to hot working taking the as-cast condition as the initial one. To do it, unidirectional hot forging at a temperature slightly below the γ' solvus temperature with a strain rate of ε' ~ 10−2 s−1 was carried out. The as-cast superalloy showed reasonable workability that allowed producing sound forgings. The forging procedure provided formation of mixed microstructure consisted of coarse non-recrystallized and fine recrystallized γ grains. EBSD analysis of the forged workpiece showed that the fraction of highangle grain boundaries was insignificant. The hot forging resulted in dynamic recovery, whereas recrystallization processes occurred only locally near the γ grain boundaries. Tensile tests were carried out for the heat treated and hot forged conditions as well as for the as-cast condition. The heat treated and especially the forged and heat treated condition showed appreciably higher strength and ductility as compared to those of the cast condition. The obtained tensile properties were compared with those of the widely applied Russian superalloy ZhS6U.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000021

From text 'andersson2011weldabilityofprecipitation pages 41-44'.

J Andersson. Weldability of precipitation hardening superalloys: influence of microstructure. Unknown journal, 2011.

The excerpt gives practical heat-treatment conditions used for homogenization, solution (sub‑/super‑solvus) treatments and grain growth for Ni superalloys (Alloy 718, Allvac 718Plus and Waspaloy). Furnace heating was 0.3 °C/s with enforced argon cooling to 500 °C and cooling rates >0.3 °C/min. For homogenization of cast Allvac 718Plus the study used 1125 °C-5 h (below γ‑Laves eutectic), 1200 °C-5 h (above eutectic), and a combined 1125 °C-5 h + 1200 °C-5 h. For Alloy 718, short/standard solution treatments were 954 °C-1 h and 982 °C-1 h (giving different amounts of δ‑phase), while long exposure at 954 °C-15 h produces large amounts of δ. Large grain sizes were produced by treatments such as 1050 °C-3 h + 954 °C-1 h and 1020 °C-1 h. Allvac 718Plus used repeated short solution steps (954 °C-0.5 h, repeated 3×) for stress relief. For Waspaloy, solution heat treatments of 996–1080 °C for 4 h were used, with the higher temperatures producing larger grain size. These documented temperatures and dwell times provide practical starting points for homogenization, sub‑/super‑solvus processing and controlled grain growth during annealing.


---

## pqac-00000022

From text 'strady2026grainstructurebehavior pages 1-2'.

C. Strady, A. Nicolay, M. Bignon, A. Agnoli, J. de Jaeger, N. Bozzolo, and M. Bernacki. Grain structure behavior of powder metallurgy nickel base γ–γ′ superalloys under γ′-supersolvus solution heat-treatment. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 57:1872-1883, Jan 2026. URL: https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-025-08107-6, doi:10.1007/s11661-025-08107-6. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt reports practical, alloy-level observations for powder-metallurgy Ni-based c–c¢ superalloys. The c¢ solvus is given as Tc¢–s. between 1100 C and 1160 C; solution heat treatment (SHT) above this solvus produces a uniform c-grain structure ~20–40 µm. Service requirements contrast bore (fine-grained, 5–10 µm) and rim (coarse-grained, 50–80 µm). c-grain evolution occurs rapidly during heating to the solution temperature and is likely governed by static recrystallization; the final grain-size distribution is set by the density of recrystallized nuclei whose boundaries consume residual work-hardened grains. Materials with limited residual work hardening can develop heterogeneous structures with abnormal grains >200 µm, which reduce fatigue resistance. Nanometric oxide ((Hf, Zr)O2) and carbide ((Nb, Ti)C) particles exert pinning pressure that stabilizes grain structure once work hardening is removed. A slower heating rate (notably the 2 C/min ramp cited) increases the likelihood of excessive grain growth on slightly work-hardened material — possibly due to ripening/dissolution of c¢ precipitates during ramp-up. Cooling rate from SHT controls subsequent c¢ precipitation (sub-solvus aging).


---

## pqac-00000023

From text 'tian2009experimentalandsimulation pages 6-7'.

Gaofeng Tian, Chengchang Jia, Jiantao Liu, and Benfu Hu. Experimental and simulation on the grain growth of p/m nickel-base superalloy during the heat treatment process. Materials & Design, 30:433-439, Mar 2009. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007, doi:10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007. This article has 71 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The paper reports practical heat-treatment conditions and simulated effects on grain growth for a PM nickel-base superalloy. They set furnace temperature ~20–40 °C above the c0 solvus (1150 C used). Transient FEM simulations examined a 1150 C treatment with holding times of 15–120 min; local point temperatures converge with time. At 60 min, bore points A–C were <1110 C while rim points D–F were 1110–1145 C; inner regions (A–C) showed slow grain growth, outer regions (E–F) rapid growth. To obtain a dual microstructure the authors recommend holding time <60 min. Monte Carlo grain-growth simulation produced region-specific grain-size grades: A,B,C = 9–10; D = 7.5–8; E = 6–6.5; F = 5.5–6. They state that keeping the disk bore temperature below ~1110 C while maintaining rim temperatures ~1130–1150 C yields coarse-grained bore (grade ~9–10) and finer rim (~5.5–6). The study emphasizes temperature gradients and hold time control for sub-/super-solvus style dual microstructure and grain growth management.


---

## pqac-00000024

From text 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 2-5'.

Yan-Xing Liu, Zhi-Jiang Ke, Run-Hua Li, Ju-Qing Song, and Jing-Jing Ruan. Study of grain growth in a ni-based superalloy by experiments and cellular automaton model. Materials, 14:6922, Nov 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/ma14226922, doi:10.3390/ma14226922. This article has 16 citations.

The study used a solution heat treatment of 1338 K for 1200 s followed by an ice-water quench to fully dissolve precipitates before grain-growth testing. Isothermal grain-growth experiments were performed at 1353–1473 K with holding times from 180 to 2400 s. The initial average grain size after solution treatment was ≈70 µm. Grain size increases with both holding time and temperature: at 1353 K the grain sizes at 600, 1500 and 2400 s were 106, 154 and 192 µm respectively; at a 540 s hold, grain sizes at 1413, 1443 and 1473 K were 160, 238 and 299 µm respectively. Grain growth follows a parabolic-type law G^n_t − G^n_0 = k_G t with k_G = k0 exp(−Q/RT). The authors identified n = 2.43 and fitted k0 and Q such that G2.43_t − G2.43_0 = 3.63 × 10^14 exp[−3.2 × 10^5/(RT)] t. Twin boundaries were merged for average-size evaluation and size was computed by an area-fraction-weighted method.


---

## pqac-00000025

From text 'tian2009experimentalandsimulation pages 1-2'.

Gaofeng Tian, Chengchang Jia, Jiantao Liu, and Benfu Hu. Experimental and simulation on the grain growth of p/m nickel-base superalloy during the heat treatment process. Materials & Design, 30:433-439, Mar 2009. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007, doi:10.1016/j.matdes.2008.06.007. This article has 71 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

This study on a powder-metallurgy nickel-base superalloy reports solution (heat) treatment in the range 950–1150 °C for 5–60 min with water quenching. A gradient/controlled solution treatment produced a dual microstructure (fine-grain bore grade ~9–10 and coarse-grain rim grade ~5.5–6). At 1010 °C grain growth with holding time is slow: grain size increased only ~4 μm when holding time increased from 5 to 45 min. Grain growth accelerates markedly between ~1110–1150 °C; at 1150 °C the grain size reached about 43 μm (grade ~5.5). Specimens were prepared from PREP powder (50–100 μm), HIP consolidated, then hot-deformed at 1090 °C (strain rate ≈5×10⁻² s⁻¹, strain =0.7) to produce a fine-grained starting microstructure before solution treatment. Grain sizes were measured by optical microscopy (image analysis). These experimental results give practical anchor points: slow growth near ~1010 °C over tens of minutes, rapid growth above ~1110 °C, and substantial coarsening by 1150 °C. The data are specific to the alloy chemistry and processing route used here (Table 1).


---

## pqac-00000026

From text 'liu2021studyofgrain pages 1-2'.

Yan-Xing Liu, Zhi-Jiang Ke, Run-Hua Li, Ju-Qing Song, and Jing-Jing Ruan. Study of grain growth in a ni-based superalloy by experiments and cellular automaton model. Materials, 14:6922, Nov 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/ma14226922, doi:10.3390/ma14226922. This article has 16 citations.

This study reports experimental and modelling data on grain growth in a commercial Ni-based superalloy. A solution (homogenization) treatment used in the experiments was 1338 K for 1200 s to dissolve precipitates. Isothermal anneals for grain growth were performed between 1353 and 1473 K with holding times from 180 to 2400 s; the grain structure continuously coarsened as holding time and temperature increased. Grain growth followed a near-parabolic law (exponent slightly above 2), indicating boundary-migration-controlled kinetics with only minor pinning by precipitates. The authors found that grain-boundary mobility increases with temperature. A cellular automaton model was developed that reproduces the dependence of grain size on holding time and temperature. The paper also cites literature noting precipitate pinning (e.g., NbC and TiN) can retard growth and that boundary morphology at high temperature influences growth behavior. These experimental temperature/time ranges and kinetic observations provide concrete reference points for homogenization and annealing-driven grain growth in a Ni-based superalloy.


---

## pqac-00000027

From text 'chavilian2025acceleratedhomogenizationof pages 2-4'.

H Chavilian and SH Razavi. Accelerated homogenization of a cast nickel-based superalloy vdm780 via thermomechanical processing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt reports thermomechanical homogenization conditions used for the cast nickel-based superalloy VDM780. STA analysis guided temperature selection, and a preheating/homogenization temperature of 1160 °C was chosen as suitable to eliminate the as-cast structure and as the inter-pass preheat. During rolling the workpiece was maintained roughly in the 1080–1120 °C range (accounting for a typical 50–60 °C drop when removed from the furnace). Heating to study heat-treatment behavior used a 10 °C/min ramp up to 1300 °C. The thermomechanical cycle involved five rolling passes on a two-high mill at a constant strain rate of 4 s⁻¹; after each pass the sample was returned to the furnace within ~10 s to encourage post-dynamic recrystallization, and samples were air-cooled after the final pass. The paper emphasizes elimination of interdendritic as-cast phases (Nb-enriched, γ/Laves-like) via homogenization. The excerpt does not provide explicit isothermal hold times for homogenization, nor general sub-solvus vs super-solvus temperature ranges or quantitative grain-growth rates during annealing, so direct broader recommendations beyond the VDM780 conditions above are not given.

From text 'moore2017graincoarseningbehaviour pages 20-23'.

I.J. Moore, J.I. Taylor, M.W. Tracy, M.G. Burke, and E.J. Palmiere. Grain coarsening behaviour of solution annealed alloy 625 between 600–800 °c. Materials Science and Engineering A-structural Materials Properties Microstructure and Processing, 682:402-409, Jan 2017. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msea.2016.11.060, doi:10.1016/j.msea.2016.11.060. This article has 67 citations.

The provided excerpt is largely a bibliography pointing to studies directly relevant to homogenization, sub‑solvus/super‑solvus heat treatments, and grain growth in nickel superalloys—especially Alloy 625. Key items include a focused study on "Grain coarsening behaviour of solution annealed alloy 625 between 600–800 °c," work on "Precipitation and recrystallization behavior of the nickel‑base alloy Inconel 625," and papers on "Microstructural evolutions during thermal aging of Alloy 625: Impact of temperature and forming process." Long‑time exposure effects are covered ("Effects of long-time exposure in Alloy 625 at 1200◦F 1400◦F and 1600◦F"), which overlap the upper range of annealing/aging temperatures. Foundational references on grain growth and boundary migration (Ralph; Higgins) and the ASTM E112 standard for grain size measurement are also cited. While the excerpt does not provide explicit procedural temperatures/times or step‑by‑step homogenization schedules, it identifies primary literature and standards that contain experimental data and discussions on temperature ranges (notably 600–800 °C and ~1200–1600 °F) and on mechanisms (precipitation, recrystallization, grain‑boundary migration) that control grain growth during annealing.

## 2. Effects of Vacuum Annealing on the Conduction Characteristics of ZnO Nanosheets
Valid Text Names:
'barnett2015effectsofvacuum pages 1-2', 'barnett2015effectsofvacuum pages 2-5', 'barnett2015effectsofvacuum pages 5-6', 'barnett2015effectsofvacuum pages 5-5'

BibTex:
@article{barnett2015effectsofvacuum,
    author = "Barnett, Chris J. and Smith, Nathan A. and Jones, Daniel R. and Maffeis, Thierry G. G. and Cobley, Richard J.",
    title = "Effects of Vacuum Annealing on the Conduction Characteristics of ZnO Nanosheets",
    year = "2015",
    journal = "Nanoscale Research Letters",
    volume = "10",
    month = "Sep",
    doi = "10.1186/s11671-015-1066-1",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1186/s11671-015-1066-1",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "1931-7573"
}


Abstract:
ZnO nanosheets are a relatively new form of nanostructure and have demonstrated potential as gas-sensing devices and dye sensitised solar cells. For integration into other devices, and when used as gas sensors, the nanosheets are often heated. Here we study the effect of vacuum annealing on the electrical transport properties of ZnO nanosheets in order to understand the role of heating in device fabrication. A low cost, mass production method has been used for synthesis and characterisation is achieved using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), photoluminescence (PL), auger electron spectroscopy (AES) and nanoscale two-point probe. Before annealing, the measured nanosheet resistance displayed a non-linear increase with probe separation, attributed to surface contamination. Annealing to 300 °C removed this contamination giving a resistance drop, linear probe spacing dependence, increased grain size and a reduction in the number of n-type defects. Further annealing to 500 °C caused the n-type defect concentration to reduce further with a corresponding increase in nanosheet resistance not compensated by any further sintering. At 700 °C, the nanosheets partially disintegrated and the resistance increased and became less linear with probe separation. These effects need to be taken into account when using ZnO nanosheets in devices that require an annealing stage during fabrication or heating during use.

function.arguments: {"question": "Vacuum induction furnace for melting and remelting steels and superalloys, vacuum level and contamination control during heat treatment", "text_name_focus_list": ["meng2023designofvacuum pages 1-2", "meng2023designofvacuum pages 2-4", "prasanna2020evaluationofproperties pages 1-2", "prasanna2020evaluationofproperties pages 2-3", "seo2020effectofmo pages 3-6"]}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000029

From text 'chavilian2025acceleratedhomogenizationof pages 2-4'.

H Chavilian and SH Razavi. Accelerated homogenization of a cast nickel-based superalloy vdm780 via thermomechanical processing. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt reports laboratory melting of the VDM780 superalloy using a vacuum induction furnace (VIM) operated at about 10⁻³ mbar. The cast ingot composition was confirmed by spark optical emission spectroscopy. STA analysis (heating at 10 °C/min up to 1300 °C) guided selection of homogenization and preheating temperatures. A preheat/homogenization temperature of 1160 °C was chosen based on STA and to allow the sample to remain in the 1080–1120 °C range during rolling. Thermomechanical processing used an electric resistance furnace with laser-pyrometer temperature control; samples were rapidly (within ~10 s) returned to the furnace after deformation to promote post-dynamic recrystallization. The process avoided ESR and VAR remelting routes. During handling a typical exit temperature drop of ~50–60 °C was observed. The excerpt documents vacuum level, temperature control, transfer timing, and analytical checks (OES, STA), but does not provide detailed contamination-control measures (e.g., gas backfilling, getter use, crucible materials, residual gas analysis) or explicit procedures to limit pickup during heat treatment.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 4-7'.

Jintao Meng, Haitao Gao, Mixue Ruan, Hai Guo, Xiaojie Zhou, and Di Zhang. Design of vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on ga-fuzzy-pid algorithm. PLOS ONE, 18:e0293823, Nov 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0293823, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0293823. This article has 14 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

This paper describes a vacuum annealing furnace (vertical internal heating) rather than an induction melting furnace, but provides useful quantitative and control-system details relevant to vacuum heat treatment. The reported working vacuum is 8 × 10–3 Pa. Typical annealing temperatures are given as about 600–1000°C (with process table entries up to 1600°C and a rated operating range 850–1400°C), target uniformity ±5°C and control accuracy ±3°C. Heat transfer in the furnace is dominated by radiation from heaters to workpieces, with some conductive and weak convective components. Heat-loss terms include conduction to furnace walls, thermal short-circuiting, and losses via instrumentation/vacuum penetrations: “heat exported from thermocouples and vacuum tubes, as well as the dissipation of heat from the observation holes.” Temperature measurement and control use multiple thermocouples (control/monitoring in three zones and nine around the workpiece area), PLC communication over RS485 to upper computer software, and a GA-optimized PID (GA-Fuzzy-PID) that outputs via PLC and a 4–20 mA link to a power regulator controlling thyristors. The paper focuses on temperature control; it notes potential leakage/heat-loss paths through penetrations that are also relevant to vacuum integrity and contamination control, but it does not provide detailed contamination-control procedures specific to induction melting/remelting.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'meng2023designofvacuum pages 2-4'.

Jintao Meng, Haitao Gao, Mixue Ruan, Hai Guo, Xiaojie Zhou, and Di Zhang. Design of vacuum annealing furnace temperature control system based on ga-fuzzy-pid algorithm. PLOS ONE, 18:e0293823, Nov 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0293823, doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0293823. This article has 14 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes a vacuum annealing furnace temperature-control system that emphasizes coordinating vacuum level and temperature to improve process outcomes and reduce contamination, energy use, and pollution. The authors propose a GA-optimized fuzzy-PID controller to tune kp, ki and kd for better temperature control. Process steps require placing the workpiece, closing the furnace, evacuating the chamber, and initiating heating only when the vacuum degree meets heating conditions. Temperature sensing uses K-type thermocouples (measurement range 0˚C–1300˚C) and typical annealing working temperatures are cited as 600–1000˚C (with furnace capability to 1200˚C). Hardware architecture centers on a Siemens S7–1200 PLC with DI/DO/AI/AO modules; setpoint temperature curves are sent from an upper computer to the PLC over RS485 using MODBUS. This information touches on vacuum initiation criteria, sensing and control hardware, and an optimization approach for PID tuning relevant to controlling contamination and thermal conditions during vacuum heat treatments.

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a table summarizing practical soak temperature and time guidance ranges for vacuum furnace annealing of steels and alloys, organized by alloy class and treatment objective (stress relief vs. full annealing vs. homogenization). Include columns for: Alloy Class, Treatment Objective, Soak Temperature Range (°C), Typical Soak Time, Grain Growth Risk Level, and Key Notes. Cover the following alloy classes:
1. Low/medium carbon steels - stress relief (~540-600°C) and full annealing (~800-900°C)  
2. Alloy steels (e.g., 4140, 4340) - stress relief and full annealing (~815-900°C)
3. Stainless steels (austenitic like 316L, PH like 17-4 PH) - stress relief and homogenization/solutionizing
4. Ni-based superalloys (e.g., Alloy 718, Waspaloy, PM alloys) - sub-solvus solution (~950-1050°C), super-solvus (~1100-1200°C), and homogenization
5. Include soak time guidance: typically 0.5-1 hr per inch of section thickness for steels, specific times from evidence for superalloys"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Alloy Class | Treatment Objective | Soak Temperature Range (°C) | Typical Soak Time | Grain Growth Risk Level | Key Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|---|---|
| Low/medium carbon steels | Stress relief / process anneal | 540–650 | 0.5–1 h/in section thickness; often ~1 h minimum after equalization | Low | Prefer the lowest temperature that achieves stress relief; staying below Ac1 minimizes recrystallization and grain coarsening. Digges notes process annealing near ~650°C and lower stress-relief around ~540°C when no further cold work is intended; vacuum heating improves surface cleanliness but soak should still be limited to the minimum needed for uniform temperature (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031) |
| Low/medium carbon steels | Full anneal | 800–900 | 0.5–1 h/in after the part reaches temperature | Medium–High | Heat about 15–30°C above the critical range, then soak only long enough for thermal equalization; longer hold increases ferrite/austenite grain size. Use the lower end for small sections and the upper end for large sections (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000009) |
| Alloy steels (e.g., 4140) | Stress relief | 540–675 | 0.5–1 h/in section thickness | Low–Medium | Keep below Ac1 to avoid unnecessary phase transformation. Vacuum processing is advantageous where scale-free surfaces or low decarburization are required; grain growth risk remains modest unless hold is excessive (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000030) |
| Alloy steels (e.g., 4140) | Full anneal | 815–845 | 0.5–1 h/in after equalization | Medium | Digges tabulates ~816–843°C for 4140-class annealing. Use shortest effective soak because grain growth depends strongly on time and temperature (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000001) |
| Alloy steels (e.g., 4340) | Full anneal | 870–900 | 0.5–1 h/in after equalization | Medium–High | Digges tabulates ~871–899°C for 4340-class annealing. Large sections may need the high end of the range, but higher soak severity increases grain-coarsening tendency (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000001) |
| Austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 316L) | Stress relief / recovery anneal | 600–800 | ~0.5–2 h depending on section size and residual stress level | Low–Medium | Favor recovery-only schedules when dimensional stability is the goal. In heavily cold-worked stainless steels, higher heating rates suppress selective grain growth and produce finer recrystallized structures; slow heating raises grain-growth risk (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000030) |
| Austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 316L) | Recrystallization / solution anneal | 800–950 for strong recrystallization; ~1050 typical solutionizing window by practice | ~0.5–1 h for sheet/light section; thicker sections need longer equalization | Medium–High | SUS316L showed annealing between 1073–1223 K (800–950°C) with finer grains at higher heating rates; once recrystallization is complete, further time at temperature promotes grain growth. Use rapid heat-up and minimum hold (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002) |
| PH stainless steels (e.g., 17-4 PH) | Stress relief | 550–650 | ~1–4 h, depending on residual stress and section | Low | Stress relief can reduce residual stress with limited coarsening. Keep below solutionizing/homogenization temperatures if grain size must be preserved (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000030) |
| PH stainless steels (e.g., 17-4 PH) | Homogenization / solutionizing | ~1050–1150 | Solutionizing ~1 h; homogenization ~1150°C for >90 min in cited cast-alloy guidance | Medium–High | For 17-4 PH analogs, homogenization at 1150°C for >90 min followed by solutionizing at 1050°C reduces segregation, but carbide coarsening still occurs and full microchemical homogenization may remain incomplete below the carbide solvus (~1250°C) (pqac-00000003) |
| Ni-based superalloys (Alloy 718) | Sub-solvus solution / stress relief | 954–982 | ~1 h typical; repeated 0.5 h steps also reported | Medium | Alloy 718 schedules of 954°C/1 h and 982°C/1 h are documented; 954°C/15 h produced much larger δ-phase content, so long holds should be avoided unless intentionally precipitating. Good choice when stress relief or controlled sub-solvus solution is needed without aggressive grain coarsening (pqac-00000021) |
| Ni-based superalloys (Waspaloy) | Sub-solvus / solution treatment | 996–1080 | ~4 h | Medium–High | Waspaloy solution treatment in this range produced larger grains at the higher temperatures. Stay at the lowest temperature consistent with dissolution/homogenization targets (pqac-00000021) |
| Ni-based superalloys (PM alloys) | Sub-solvus solution | 950–1050 | ~5–60 min; often ≤45–60 min | Low–Medium below ~1010°C; Medium near 1050°C | PM superalloy data show slow grain growth near ~1010°C, with only small size increase from 5 to 45 min. This is the preferred regime when stress relief/homogenization is needed with minimal coarsening (pqac-00000025) |
| Ni-based superalloys (PM alloys) | Near-/super-solvus solution | 1100–1150 | Typically 15–60 min; keep <60 min when dual microstructure or limited coarsening is required | High | Grain growth accelerates sharply above ~1110°C. A furnace set ~20–40°C above the γ′ solvus and hold times beyond ~60 min caused rapid coarsening, especially in hotter regions; temperature uniformity is critical (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000023) |
| Ni-based superalloys (cast 718Plus / similar) | Homogenization | 1125–1200 | ~5 h per step; combined 1125°C/5 h + 1200°C/5 h also reported | High | Effective for reducing segregation, but grain growth and eutectic/Laves-related risks rise rapidly as temperature increases. Use only where chemistry requires it, and verify against incipient-melting limits and segregation state (pqac-00000021) |
| Ni-based superalloys (cast VDM780-like) | Homogenization / preheat | ~1160 | Alloy-specific; evidence supports the temperature but not a general soak time | High | VIM-produced VDM780 used ~10^-3 mbar melting and selected 1160°C from STA as a homogenization/preheat temperature. Good example that homogenization temperature should be tied to thermal analysis and segregation morphology, not a generic rule (pqac-00000029) |
| Ni-based superalloys (general grain-growth kinetics reference) | Isothermal anneal after solutionizing | 1080–1200 | 180–2400 s in experiments | Medium–Very High | Experimental Ni-superalloy grain growth rises strongly with both temperature and time: e.g., after solution treatment, grain size increased substantially between 1353–1473 K and 180–2400 s. This supports a best practice of shortest possible hold and avoiding unnecessary exposure above solvus (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000026) |


*Table: This table summarizes practical soak temperature and time ranges for vacuum furnace annealing of steels and nickel-based alloys, organized by alloy class and treatment objective. It also flags relative grain-growth risk and notes the main control levers needed to achieve stress relief or homogenization without excessive coarsening.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031]

Status: Paper Count=48 | Relevant Papers=18 | Clinical Trial Count=0 | Relevant Clinical Trials=0 | Current Evidence=32 | Current Cost=$0.2635 | Disease-Target Evidence=0

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a table summarizing best practices for controlling grain growth during vacuum furnace annealing of steels and alloys. Include columns for: Strategy, Mechanism/Rationale, Practical Guidance, and Applicable Alloy Systems. Cover the following strategies:
1. Minimize soak temperature - use lowest temperature that achieves treatment objective
2. Minimize soak time - match soak time to section thickness using established rules
3. Control heating rate - faster heating rates produce finer recrystallized grains
4. Microalloying with Nb, Ti, V, Mo - Zener pinning by fine carbide/nitride precipitates
5. Hollomon-Jaffe parameter optimization - use HP to combine temperature and time effects
6. Sub-solvus vs super-solvus selection - stay below solvus when possible
7. Precipitate size and volume fraction control - smaller particles more effective at pinning
8. Vacuum level and temperature uniformity - maintain proper vacuum and minimize thermal gradients
9. Cooling rate control - manage precipitation during cooling"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| Strategy | Mechanism/Rationale | Practical Guidance | Applicable Alloy Systems |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimize soak temperature | Grain growth rate rises strongly with temperature because grain-boundary mobility increases; full anneals and near-/above-solvus treatments promote much more coarsening than recovery/stress-relief cycles (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000026) | Use the lowest temperature that still achieves the objective: for steel stress relief stay subcritical when possible; for stainless and Ni alloys prefer recovery or sub-solvus schedules unless segregation or phase-dissolution requirements force higher temperatures (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000025) | Carbon and alloy steels, stainless steels, PH stainless steels, Ni-based superalloys |
| Minimize soak time | Time at temperature adds directly to grain coarsening; even when temperature is fixed, longer holds increase grain size and can coarsen pinning precipitates (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025) | For steels, use only the time needed for thermal equalization, commonly about 0.5–1 h/in section thickness; for Ni alloys keep high-temperature holds as short as practical, often tens of minutes rather than hours near the solvus unless homogenization specifically requires longer exposure (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000023) | Steels, stainless steels, Ni-based superalloys |
| Control heating rate | Faster heating increases nucleation density during recrystallization and reduces the time spent in the temperature window where selective grain growth can occur; slow heating permits preferential growth of some grains (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000022) | Ramp as quickly as part geometry and distortion limits allow through the recrystallization range; avoid unnecessarily slow ramps for cold-worked stainless steels and PM superalloys, especially near solvus temperatures where abnormal grains can form during prolonged heat-up (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000022) | Cold-worked stainless steels, PM Ni superalloys, heavily deformed alloys |
| Microalloying with Nb, Ti, V, Mo | Fine carbides/nitrides/carbonitrides pin grain boundaries by Zener pinning and solute drag; Nb, Ti, V, and Mo improve resistance to abnormal grain growth, especially in austenite (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014) | Where chemistry can be selected, use Nb/Ti/V microalloying for steels and Nb+Mo combinations for high-temperature vacuum-carburizing or annealing steels; maintain precipitates fine and numerous rather than relying on coarse particles (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000020) | Microalloyed steels, vacuum-carburizing steels, austenitic stainless steels |
| Hollomon-Jaffe parameter optimization | Temperature and time act together; the Hollomon-Jaffe parameter provides a compact way to compare schedules and avoid drifting from recovery/stress relief into recrystallization and grain growth (pqac-00000005) | For microalloyed steels, keep the parameter in the recovery-dominant window when stress relief is the goal; avoid HP values associated with full recrystallization and grain growth unless refinement via recrystallization is desired (pqac-00000005) | Steels, especially microalloyed and cold-worked strip/product forms |
| Sub-solvus vs super-solvus selection | Below the solvus, undissolved precipitates remain to pin boundaries; above the solvus, pinning is lost and grain growth accelerates sharply (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000023) | Stay sub-solvus whenever stress relief or moderate homogenization is sufficient; move to near-/super-solvus only when segregation removal or phase dissolution requires it, and then strictly limit hold time and thermal gradients (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000023) | Ni-based superalloys, PH alloys, some stainless and tool steels with stable precipitates |
| Precipitate size and volume fraction control | Limiting grain size scales roughly with particle size divided by particle volume fraction; smaller, more numerous particles give stronger pinning, and elongated/coherent particles can be even more effective (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000020) | Design process routes to preserve fine precipitates: avoid overlong holds that coarsen them, exploit lower-temperature precipitation where feasible, and recognize that coarse solidification precipitates are usually less effective than fine solid-state precipitates (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000020) | Microalloyed steels, precipitation-strengthened stainless steels, Ni superalloys |
| Vacuum level and temperature uniformity | Vacuum limits oxidation/decarburization and contamination, but grain control still depends on uniform temperature; local hot spots and radial gradients produce localized rapid grain growth (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031) | Heat only after the target vacuum is achieved; maintain stable vacuum, good thermocouple coverage, and tight uniformity. Typical reported practice includes high vacuum for annealing and minimizing losses/leaks through penetrations. Avoid load arrangements that create rim-to-core temperature differences near critical temperatures (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000023) | All vacuum-annealed steels and alloys; especially large sections and PM/cast superalloys |
| Cooling rate control | Cooling affects re-precipitation, retained phase fractions, and post-soak coarsening; too slow a cool can allow unwanted precipitation or continued microstructural coarsening, while controlled cooling can preserve the intended sub-solvus/supersolvus result (pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022) | After high-temperature holds, cool at the rate required by the alloy system: enforce sufficiently fast cooling from solution temperatures in Ni alloys and PH stainless steels to prevent unwanted precipitation during descent, but tailor the cool to avoid distortion or cracking in steels (pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000003) | PH stainless steels, Ni-based superalloys, alloy steels |
| Tie cycle design to section size and segregation severity | Thick sections need more time to equalize, but excess dwell beyond equalization only increases coarsening; heavily segregated cast structures may justify harsher homogenization than wrought or PM materials (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000027) | Use mild stress-relief schedules for wrought parts and reserve long high-temperature homogenization cycles for castings or severely segregated material. Validate temperature selection with thermal analysis/solvus data rather than using one generic recipe across alloys (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000029) | Wrought steels, cast stainless steels, cast and wrought Ni-based superalloys |


*Table: This table summarizes evidence-based strategies for limiting grain coarsening during vacuum furnace annealing while still achieving stress relief or homogenization. It is useful as a process-planning checklist across steels, stainless steels, and Ni-based alloys.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00, artifact-01]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031]

Status: Paper Count=48 | Relevant Papers=18 | Clinical Trial Count=0 | Relevant Clinical Trials=0 | Current Evidence=32 | Current Cost=$0.2635 | Disease-Target Evidence=0
