snippets: Full annealing is a softening process in which a steel is heated to a temperature above the transformation range (Acs) and, after being held for a sufficient time at this temperature, is cooled slowly to a temperature below the transformation
The temperature range normally used for full annealing is 25 to 50 deg F above this line (the upper critical), as shown in figure 11.
A rule frequently used is to soak % hr/in. of thickness.
Process annealing, frequently termed stress-relief annealing, is usually applied to cold-worked low carbon steels (up to about 0.25% of carbon)
The steel is usually heated close to, but below, the Aci temperature (fig. 11).
If the steel is not to be further cold-worked, but relief of internal stresses is desired, a lower range of temperature will suffice (about 1,000° F).
This procedure is also advantageous when treating steels having considerable variations in section thickness or very low thermal conductivity.
The object of holding a steel at heat-treating temperature is to assure uniformity of temperature throughout its entire volume.
B, Same as (A) after process annealing at 1,200° F.
C, Same as (A) after full annealing at 1,650° F.
Spheroidizing is a process of heating and cooling steel that produces a rounded or globular form ... accomplished by prolonged heating at temperatures just below the Aci (fig. 11)
The final step, however, should consist of holding at a temperature just below the critical (Ari).
All traces of cold working are eliminated, and the ferrite grains are larger than in (B).

contexts.context: This study on a Ni-based superalloy used a solution treatment of 1338 K for 1200 s followed by ice-water quench to dissolve precipitates. Grain-growth experiments were then run isothermally at holding temperatures between 1353 and 1473 K with holding times from 180 to 2400 s. The initial average grain size was about 70 µm. Measured grain sizes increased with both temperature and time: at 1353 K, grain sizes were 106 µm (600 s), 154 µm (1500 s) and 192 µm (2400 s); at 540 s holding time, increasing temperature gave 160 µm (1413 K), 238 µm (1443 K) and 299 µm (1473 K). Grain growth follows a parabolic law; for this alloy the fitted relationship was G2.43t − G2.430 = 3.63×1014 exp[ −3.2×105/(RT)] t (n ≈ 2.43, fitted k0 and activation energy Q ≈ 3.2×105 J/mol). Implications: higher soak temperatures and longer soak times strongly accelerate grain growth, so controlling grain growth requires reducing either temperature or time (or both) and tailoring process parameters to the alloy-specific kinetics.
contexts.question: What are the recommended soak temperatures and times for vacuum annealing of steels and alloys for stress relief and homogenization, and how to control grain growth?

contexts.context: The excerpt outlines basic heat-treating practice: a time–temperature cycle composed of heating, holding (soaking), and cooling. It emphasizes that heating rate is generally not critical except for parts with high residual or cold-work stresses or prior hardening, where "The rate of heating should be slow^." It warns that introducing cold steel into a hot furnace can "cause distortion or even cracking," indicating the need to control thermal gradients when loading furnaces. The text also notes composition effects: "The temperature of formation of bainite is not appreciably affected by carbon content, but the time for its formation increases with the carbon," and that "Both the Mg and Mf temperatures are markedly lowered by increasing carbon content," which implies soak/transform schedules must account for alloy composition. The excerpt does not provide numeric soak temperatures, soak times, vacuum-specific procedures, or explicit grain-growth-prevention techniques; it provides general principles (cycle phases, cautious heating for stressed parts, risk of thermal shock, and composition-dependent transformation behavior) relevant to planning anneal/homogenization cycles.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports a simulation and experimental study of gradient heat treatment of a P/M nickel-base superalloy disk. The authors used a furnace temperature 20–40°C above the c0 solvus (1150°C in this study). Temperature was monitored at six radial points; with increasing hold time the radial temperature gradient reduced and temperatures tended toward homogeneity. At a 60 min hold the inner points A–C were below 1110°C while outer points D–F were 1110–1145°C, implying inner regions grow more slowly and outer regions can grow rapidly. To achieve a dual microstructure the authors recommend holding time should be less than 60 min. They note a treatment window where bore <1110°C and rim 1130–1150°C yields fine/coarse region grain grades (bore ~9–10, rim ~5.5–6). Heat transfer in their model included convection and radiation between disk, furnace wall and furnace air. Monte Carlo simulation was used to model grain growth. These points inform soak temperature selection relative to solvus, the importance of radial temperature control and limiting hold time to reduce undesirable grain growth during homogenization/stress-relief.

contexts.context: The excerpt provides annealing guidance relevant to controlling recovery, recrystallization and grain growth in steels that can inform vacuum annealing schedules. It emphasizes using the Hollomon–Jaffe (HP) parameter to combine temperature and time into a single metric (HP = T·[log(t)+20]·10^-3 as presented) and reports empirical HP ranges: recovery for HP ≈ 16–19.3, partial microstructures in the intermediate range, and full recrystallization and grain growth when HP > 20.5. Microalloying (especially solute niobium and molybdenum) strongly retards recovery/recrystallization and widens the stable recovery window, while precipitation (e.g., 50–60 pct of available Nb) or raising peak temperatures to promote precipitation reduces solute drag and can permit grain growth. Practical observations include that 0.04 wt% Nb steel required ~80× longer at 700 °C to reach 95% recrystallization, and that in continuous lines short cycle times (≈120 s) may force peak temperatures toward ~800 °C. Lower coiling/soak temperatures retain microalloy in solution and give finer grains; the achievable recrystallized grain size limit is ≈3 µm. Overall, select soak T–t to keep HP below recrystallization thresholds, coordinate with alloy solute/precipitation behavior, and use microalloying/precipitation control to prevent unwanted grain growth.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses vacuum carburizing at elevated temperatures (up to ~1100°C) to shorten cycle times and increase productivity, but it warns that higher carburizing temperatures promote austenite grain growth, which can degrade fatigue performance. To limit austenite grain coarsening during carburizing, microalloying to form small carbo-nitride precipitates is effective. Nb additions alone provide some control of abnormal austenite grain growth, while Mo combined with Nb gives enhanced resistance to grain growth, particularly at high carburizing temperatures up to ~1050°C; this enhanced control is attributed to solute and precipitation effects. The paper also describes two pre-heat treatment approaches: (1) austenitizing with isothermal annealing to alter ferrite/pearlite morphology and (2) a subcritical anneal to soften by annealing ferrite and bainite. The excerpt contrasts conventional gas carburizing temperatures (920–940°C) with vacuum carburizing and notes vacuum processing advantages (reduced intergranular oxidation, cleaner surfaces, compressed gas quenching) and that compressed gas quenching may require higher hardenability. These points are relevant to strategies for avoiding grain growth during high-temperature vacuum furnace treatments.

contexts.context: The excerpt reviews how microalloying (notably niobium, titanium, vanadium, molybdenum) influences recovery, recrystallization, phase transformation, and grain growth during downstream thermal processing. Key points relevant to annealing and grain-growth control: thermal cycle durations used downstream span seconds to tens of hours and heating/cooling rates can be extremely fast or very slow, so soak times and rates strongly affect microalloy state. Precipitation in hot austenite is relatively slow and follows a C‑curve with a nose around 900–930 °C, meaning time/temperature windows exist where precipitate nucleation/growth is most rapid. Ti and Nb are strong carbide/nitride formers with low solubility, and several microalloy atoms are larger than iron, promoting segregation to boundaries/dislocations; these attributes (plus solute drag and precipitate pinning quantified by the SRP/TNR concepts) retard recrystallization and grain growth. Deformation accelerates particle nucleation, altering precipitate evolution during subsequent anneals. These mechanistic observations imply that controlling soak temperature relative to precipitation kinetics, tailoring heating/cooling rates, and leveraging microalloy chemistry/precipitates are central strategies to achieve stress relief/homogenization while preventing excessive grain growth.

contexts.context: The excerpt provides general annealing guidance for steels (not vacuum-specific): choose the upper annealing temperature range for large sections and the lower range for small sections. Typical soak (time at temperature after heating uniformly through) is roughly 1 hour for thin carbon or low-alloy sections and up to about 4 hours for heavy sections of high-alloy steel. Normalizing/annealing cooling is typically done in still air. The text also indicates recommended limits for cooling rates after annealing/normalizing — roughly not to exceed about 75°F/hr for carbon and low-alloy steels and about 50°F/hr for high-alloy steels. The excerpt mainly addresses conventional furnace practice and tool/stainless-steel groups; it does not provide vacuum-furnace-specific soak temperatures or times for stress relief versus homogenization, nor specific guidance for nickel alloys or detailed grain-growth prevention techniques (e.g., low soak temperatures, shorter soaks, controlled ramp rates, use of microalloying, or recrystallization/precipitation treatments).

contexts.context: This work on additively manufactured (AM) 17-4 PH stainless steel reports that standard industry stress-relief heat treatments have little effect on the as-built dendritic microstructure and high retained-austenite fraction (nearly 50%). Implementing a homogenization heat-treatment regimen (specifically the Aerospace Materials Specification 5355 for CB7Cu-1, a casting analog) eliminated the dendritic solidification structure and produced a substantially martensitic microstructure (~90% martensite, ~10% retained austenite). The paper notes conventional annealing/aging ranges in literature around 400 °C to 650 °C with times spanning 5 minutes to 5000 hours. Experimental details indicate the AM builds used nitrogen as purging gas, which is relevant because powder/atmosphere choices can influence retained austenite and microstructure. Overall, the excerpt supports that (1) simple stress-relief cycles may not homogenize AM microstructures, (2) higher-temperature homogenization (per AMS 5355) can remove dendritic segregation and convert microstructure toward martensite, and (3) atmosphere and processing parameters affect phase constitution. These points are relevant when choosing vacuum-furnace anneal soak temperatures and times and when planning measures to limit grain growth during homogenization.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses how annealing temperature and time control precipitation, solute drag, and grain-boundary pinning in microalloyed steels, which is directly relevant to stress-relief/homogenization and grain-growth control. It notes that precipitation kinetics accelerate at higher annealing temperatures—"completeness of precipitation is achieved in a shorter time period at higher annealing temperature"—and that particle size grows with longer isothermal holds, especially "when annealing above the Curie temperature due to the significantly better diffusivity of niobium in paramagnetic ferrite." Smaller second-phase particles are more effective at Zener pinning ("For the same second-phase volume fraction, smaller particle sizes are more effective in pinning grain-boundary motion"), while elongated particles can be particularly effective at retarding grain growth ("second-phase particles with an elongated morphology are more effective in retarding grain growth"). For austenite, microalloy precipitation is slow and follows a C-curve with a nose near 900–930 C, so precipitates often do not reach completeness during industrial austenite conditioning. The text also emphasizes the role of solute drag ("solute drag has a relatively more significant contribution to austenite conditioning") and that the overall "time–temperature schedule ... determines the microstructural features." These points inform decisions on soak temperature/time trade-offs: higher temperature shortens required soak for precipitation and homogenization but promotes coarsening/grain growth unless pinned by fine or elongated second-phase particles or by solute drag; alloy content and process (batch vs continuous) strongly affect outcomes.

contexts.context: The excerpt documents heat-treatment behavior of additively manufactured 17-4 PH stainless steel relevant to stress relief and homogenization. For cast/alloy analogs, AMS 5355 recommends a homogenization heat treatment at 1150 C for >90 min followed by solutionizing at 1050 C to reduce segregation and approach a more uniform microstructure. The MC carbide solvus lies above ~1250 C, so treatments below this (including 1050 C solutionizing) will not fully dissolve MC carbides, explaining observed carbide coarsening after solutionizing. At 1050 C the equilibrium is FCC-Austenite + MC niobium carbides. Stress-relieving (EOS) treatments increased hardness (reported 312 ± 17 VHN) while still retaining some FCC/austenite; more aggressive anneals (AM-S) produced predominantly martensite and reduced retained austenite to ~15–20% but niobium carbides still coarsened, indicating anneal times/temperatures were insufficient to fully homogenize microchemistry. The text also notes microsegregation is present and that multiple tempering cycles cause "coarsening of carbides," while cryogenic treatments (dry ice) can modify secondary carbide populations. These points indicate temperature thresholds and limits for carbide dissolution and homogenization, and highlight trade-offs between hardness, retained phases, and carbide coarsening.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports three conclusions from an experimental/simulation study on a powder-metallurgy (P/M) nickel-base superalloy. First, the authors established relationships between grain size and solution temperature and between grain size and solution holding time for this alloy. Second, they successfully simulated a microstructure having different grain sizes in different regions using a finite element model. Third, they demonstrated that a dual microstructure can be produced in a disk-shaped part by controlling local treating temperatures: keeping the disk bore temperature below 1110 C (9–10 grade) while holding the disk rim at about 1130–1150 C (5.5–6 grade). These points imply that regional temperature control during solution treatment can produce non-uniform (dual) grain structures and that both temperature and soak time govern grain size evolution. The excerpt does not provide specific vacuum-furnace soak times, stress-relief cycles, homogenization procedures, or general grain-growth-prevention techniques for steels or other nickel alloys, so its direct applicability to general vacuum-anneal guidelines is limited.
contexts.question: Vacuum furnace annealing soak temperature time guidelines for stress relief homogenization of steel and nickel alloys, grain growth prevention techniques

contexts.context: The excerpt addresses heat treatments for cast irons and gives specific temperature/time ranges that may provide limited guidance for stress relief and homogenization concepts in other alloys. For relieving residual stresses (stress relieving/aging/mild annealing) it recommends heating cast iron between 800° and 1,100° F, holding 30 minutes to 5 hours, then slow furnace cooling. For softening/malleabilizing, it cites higher ranges: common softening anneal 1,400°–1,500° F (with some uses at 1,200°–1,250° F), malleabilizing at about 1,500°–1,650° F with very long holds (often 2–3 days). Nodular iron can be made ferritic by heating at about 1,300° F for 1–5 hours. The text also notes that alloys containing carbide-forming elements (Cr, Mo, V, Mn) resist softening and require higher annealing temperatures. These values apply to cast irons and are not direct prescriptions for tool steels, stainless steels, or nickel-based superalloys in vacuum furnaces, but the stress-relief range and the influence of alloying on required temperatures are relevant considerations when developing vacuum furnace cycles for those materials.
contexts.question: Specific temperature ranges for stress relief annealing and homogenization annealing of tool steels, stainless steels, and nickel-based superalloys in vacuum furnaces

snippets: The heat treatment that relieves residual stresses is commonly called "aging," "normalizing," or "mild annealing;" the term "stress relieving" is more accurately descriptive.  This treatment can be accomplished by heating the cast iron to between 800° and 1,100° F, holding at temperature from 30 min. to 5 hr, the time depending on the size and temperature, and cooling slowly in the furnace.
The temperatures used in malleabilizing usually range from about 1,500° to 1,650° F. Time at temperature is quite long, frequently being as much as two to three days.
The most commonly used for this purpose is 1,400° to 1,500° F, although temperatures as low as 1,200° to 1,250° F have been used satisfactorily.

The matrix of nodular iron can be made completely ferritic by heating at 1,300° F for 1 to 5 hr, depending on composition, size of casting, and amount of pearlite in the as-cast structure; cooling from the annealing temperature can be at any convenient rate.

Irons that contain carbide-forming elements (such as chromium, molybdenum, vanadium, and manganese) are more resistant to softening than the ordinary gray iron and, therefore, must be annealed at considerably higher temperatures.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports homogenization/hot-working practice for the nickel-based superalloy VDM780 produced by vacuum induction melting. STA analysis (heated to 1300 °C at 10 °C/min) was used to select process temperatures. A preheating/homogenization temperature of 1160 °C was chosen because STA indicated it would eliminate the as-cast structure and serve as the inter-pass preheat for rolling. During forming the sample temperature was maintained in the range of 1080–1120 °C (accounting for an approximate 50–60 °C drop after exiting the furnace). Processing was performed in a vacuum induction furnace at ~10⁻³ mbar, and thermomechanical cycles included rapid transfer back to the furnace between passes to enable post-dynamic recrystallization. The paper therefore provides specific homogenization/preheat temperatures and vacuum level for a Ni-base superalloy but contains no specific temperature ranges for stress-relief annealing nor for homogenization/stress relief of tool steels or stainless steels.
contexts.question: Specific temperature ranges for stress relief annealing and homogenization annealing of tool steels, stainless steels, and nickel-based superalloys in vacuum furnaces

contexts.context: The excerpt is Table 6 from Digges & Rosenberg (1966) listing recommended heat-treatment temperatures (normalizing, annealing, hardening) and quenching media for many AISI/SAE steels. Example table entries show annealing ranges typically in the ~1,400–1,660°F region (for example, SAE 1040 anneal 1,476/1,526°F; SAE 4140 anneal 1,500/1,550°F). The authors note that no tempering temperatures are given because those depend on desired hardness, that inclusion of normalizing and annealing temperatures does not imply other treatments, and that when different quenching media are suggested smaller sections may harden satisfactorily in slower media. They also advise using the upper end of the heat-treating ranges for large sections. The table covers many carbon and alloy steels but does not explicitly identify stress-relief anneal ranges, homogenization (solution or homogenization anneal) treatments for cast or wrought alloys, vacuum-furnace practices, or nickel‑base superalloys. Therefore this excerpt provides general annealing-temperature ranges for conventional steels but lacks specific guidance for stress-relief or homogenization anneals in vacuum for tool steels, stainless steels, or Ni‑based superalloys.

contexts.context: The excerpt addresses tempering and stress-relief practices rather than broad annealing/homogenization schedules for all alloy classes. It emphasizes that tempering requires adequate soak time: short tempering periods are generally undesirable and "Good practice requires at least   hr (or, preferably, 1 to 2 lir) at tempering temperature for any hardened steel." Hardness falls rapidly at first during tempering and then more slowly with time, and tempering should be done promptly after hardening to avoid cracking caused by retained austenite transforming to fresh martensite (which may require double tempering). For normalized structural steels, the normalizing operation is often followed by heating to about 1,200°–1,300°F to relieve internal stresses. The excerpt also includes an example of a case-hardening (carburizing) schedule: ~0.030–0.050 in. case in about 4 hr at 1,700°F. Overall, the passage gives general guidance on temper/soak duration (on the order of 0.5–2 hours at temperature for hardened steels) and a stress-relief/tempering range for normalized structural steels (≈1,200–1,300°F), but does not provide specific anneal/homogenization temperature ranges or per-inch soak-time rules for tool steels, carbon steels, stainless steels, or superalloys.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt gives general guidance for annealing but does not explicitly separate stress‑relief and homogenization temperatures for the alloy classes requested. It states that the upper annealing temperature range should be used for large sections and the lower limit for small sections. Soak (time at temperature after heating uniformly through) is given as roughly 1 hour for thin carbon or low‑alloy sections, and up to about 4 hours for heavy high‑alloy sections; no explicit hours per inch of thickness (hr/in) are provided. Cooling through the transformation range is limited to about 75°F/hr for carbon and low‑alloy steels and about 50°F/hr for high‑alloy steels. Fragments from Table 7 suggest annealing/normalizing temperature bands in the neighborhood of "1,450/1,700"°F and "1,375/1,450"°F for some tool steels, but the table context is incomplete in the excerpt. In short, the excerpt supplies general soak‑time ranges and cooling‑rate limits but lacks precise, separate annealing temperatures for stress relief versus homogenization for tool steels, stainless steels, carbon steels, and superalloys, and it does not give a reliable soak time per inch value. Additional references are needed for the exact temperature bands and hr/in guidance per alloy class.

contexts.context: The excerpt does not provide annealing temperature ranges or soak times for stress relief or homogenization. It instead discusses hardening, tempering, and hardenability. It notes that carbon steels are "essentially shallow-hardening" and "cannot be hardened throughout even in moderate (about %-in.) cross sections," typically requiring severe quench media (water or brine). Alloying elements increase hardenability—"displacing the S-curve to the right"—so alloy structural steels "can be hardened throughout in larger sections and, generally, by means of a milder quenching medium, such as oil." Tempering ranges given are: alloy steels "usually tempered at temperatures of 500° to 700° F or even higher," while higher-carbon/tool steels are tempered at "300° to 500° F." The excerpt also notes that carbide-forming elements (vanadium, molybdenum, chromium) "are very effective in inhibiting grain growth in austenite." Because the passage focuses on hardening/tempering behavior rather than annealing, it lacks the requested annealing (stress-relief or homogenization) temperature ranges and soak-time-per-inch guidance for tool steels, carbon steels, stainless steels, or superalloys.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses Zener-type pinning of austenite grain boundaries by Nb-containing precipitates and the roles of particle size (r) and volume fraction (f). Only particles <50 nm were used for pinning-force calculations because these fine precipitates are effective at boundary pinning. Theoretical calculations assume all Nb is in precipitates (NbC or NbN). Driving forces (Fd) were evaluated using Zener and Gladman expressions (Fd = 2γ/D form) and pinning forces (Fp) using Zener-based models in rigid boundary (RBM) and flexible boundary (FBM) forms; the equations show Fp scales with f, γ and inversely with r (different prefactors for Eqs. (5)–(7)). Parameters used include γ = 0.8 J/m2 and size advantage Z = 2. TEM-measured mean radii (Table 3) and volume fractions (Table 4) were used (e.g. for 0.28Nb f ≈ 0.29/0.26/0.308; for 0.45Nb f ≈ 0.50/0.50/0.495). Comparisons of Fd and Fp indicate that higher Nb (≈0.28 wt.% and above) yields sufficient pinning (especially in FBM) to prevent grain coarsening at 1373 K, while lower Nb steels show some grain growth. Equating Fd and Fp implies a limiting grain size that increases with particle radius and decreases with increasing particle volume fraction (D_lim roughly ∝ r/f).
contexts.question: Zener pinning of austenite grain boundaries by precipitates, effect of particle size and volume fraction on limiting grain size during annealing

The assumption for the theoretical calculations is that all Nb is bound in precipitates, either in NbC or NbN.

Various models for the calculation of driving and pinning forces have been proposed in the literature. All of them are modifications of Zener’s original expressions.46) Here Eqs. (3)–(7) were used for the analyses, the expressions of Zener and Gladman (Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively) for calculating the driving forces and the Zener expressions (Eq. (5)), i.e. rigid boundary model (RBM) and flexible boundary model (FBM) (Eqs. (6)–(7), respectively) for the calculation of the pinning forces.16,47)

The driving forces predicted by Gladman’s expression are lower than those predicted by Zener’s expression, because Gladman takes account for the size advantage of the growing grain in a matrix, whereas Zener presented the value of 2 in his equation.16)

In the Equations, γ is the grain boundary energy (taken as 0.8 J/m2),18) D the average radius of the grains, Z the size advantage, taken as 2,  f the volume fraction of particles (taken from Table 4) and r the radius of the spherical particles.
As seen from Fig. 8, Nb precipitates are approximately spherical so there is no need to convert their size to obtain the radius.

The RBM for the pinning force assumes that the motion of a rigid boundary is capable of interacting only with those particles lying within ± r of the boundary plane (r is the radius of the particle). The FBM presumes that an infinitely flexible boundary can interact with every particle of
The results of the calculations of both forces are shown in Fig. 11.

According to Fig. 11, the driving force in the 0.11Nb steel is slightly higher than the pinning force, and consistently we see from Fig. 5(c) that some grain coarsening takes place at 1 373 K, but hardly at lower temperatures.
Hence, the Nb micro-alloying of 0.28 wt.% seems to be appropriate to achieve the maximum efficiency of the alloying.

contexts.context: The authors evaluate Zener-type pinning of austenite grain boundaries by precipitates using several models (Zener, rigid boundary model (RBM), flexible boundary model (FBM)) and driving-force expressions (Zener and Gladman). Assumptions used include Gladman Z = 2 and an interfacial energy of 0.8 J/m2; a maximum grain size of 1600 µm was used for driving-force calculations. Because Ti-rich carbonitrides are rectangular rather than spherical, an equivalent radius was computed to use spherical-particle expressions, and precipitate volume fraction was estimated by thermodynamic calculation. Calculations show FBM predicts larger pinning forces than Zener or RBM (more particles interacting with boundaries). Comparing pinning (Fp) and driving (Fd) forces indicates driving forces exceed pinning only for smaller grains (<600 µm); for larger grains pinning forces are larger (values close to each other), implying an equilibrium between grain coarsening and precipitate coarsening. The strong pinning by Ti-rich carbonitrides below 1250 ◦C inhibits grain growth (fine and coarse austenite) and abnormal grain growth would be expected only at higher temperatures.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses Zener-type pinning of austenite grain boundaries by second-phase particles, emphasizing that limiting grain size during annealing is controlled by particle volume fraction (f) and mean particle size (R). Maximum grain refinement is achieved with a high volume fraction and the smallest possible mean particle size; particles formed at lower temperatures (solid-state precipitation) are most effective, while those formed during solidification tend to be coarser and less effective. Simulations and analyses reported show a stronger inhibition of grain growth than classic Zener/Smith random-interaction predictions, with the fraction of particle–boundary interactions and the dependence of pinned grain size on f being larger than 2D expectations. TiN in microalloyed steels is given as a case study: TiN precipitates as fine, stable cuboids with low solubility, making it particularly effective at pinning and resistant to coarsening. Particle coarsening during thermal exposure follows the Lifshitz–Wagner kinetics, so lower dissolved solute (e.g., [Ti]) and faster cooling reduce coarsening and improve pinning efficacy. Overall, smaller R, larger f, low solute levels and minimized coarsening give the smallest limiting grain size.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses numerical mean-field modeling of particle pinning (Zener pinning) and its effect on limiting grain size during annealing. Zener parameters (K and m) from Hillert (K = 0.222, m = 0.93) were used to compute a Zener radius (about 8 lm), but numerical results indicate adjustments are needed because the simulated ratio of Rm to RCr (~0.5–0.6) is lower than the 0.75 assumed in prior theory. For the special case Rm = 0.5 RCr, the model predicts RCr = Rz (the Zener radius). Simulations show strong dependence on initial grain size: small initial grains (0.2 lm) produce discontinuous, stepwise growth with multiple plateaus as precipitates nucleate/coarsen and pin grain boundaries; larger initial grains (3.2 lm) show continuous but slowed growth and deviation from parabolic kinetics. The model uses an Arrhenius grain-boundary mobility (M = M0 exp(-Q/RT)) and a calibrated mobility giving ~7.94e-16 m4/Js at 722 °C. Overall, particle size, volume fraction (via Zener radius), and initial grain size control whether growth is arrested near the Zener radius or continues with reduced kinetics.

contexts.context: The excerpt gives a Zener-type relationship for the limiting grain size D set by precipitate pinning: D = (1/K) · d / f (equation 9.9), i.e. limiting grain size scales directly with particle size d and inversely with particle volume fraction f, with K an efficiency factor. K depends on particle shape, crystallographic orientation and particle-matrix coherency: cubic particles generally exert larger drag than spheres, and coherent precipitates provide the highest resistance to boundary migration. For spheres a form used is K = 3.(1 + cos y0)/2. The pressures in Table 9.1 are maximum values based on assumptions (all particles similarly oriented) and an approximation for surface density; orientation distributions should be averaged if present. The authors note the coherency degree alters pinning (plots shown in figures 9.5–9.6) and that the quantitative equations could be improved with better statistical treatment, but the approximations give reasonable order-of-magnitude results. Measurements discussed concern Ti-microalloyed steel prior-austenite grains.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses Zener (Zener/Smith) pinning of austenite grain boundaries by TiN precipitates and emphasizes that both particle volume fraction and particle size strongly control limiting grain size during annealing. Experimental data (Pickering) show clear effects of TiN volume fraction and particle size on austenite grain size. Maintaining small, coarsening-resistant TiN particles requires low dissolved Ti in austenite and thus relatively high dissolved N at a given austenitising temperature; this condition is favored by a hypostoichiometric composition. Rapid solidification (e.g., continuous casting) refines TiN formed during solidification, permitting more hypostoichiometry. However, predictability of limiting grain size using a simple Zener/Smith relation is limited because particle shape (non-spherical), coarsening behavior, interactions with other microalloying elements (V, Nb), and solute levels complicate the microstructure. Related models for recrystallization (Cotterill) show that reducing interparticle spacing transitions behavior from accelerated to severely retarded recrystallization, indicating that pinning pressure and kinetics depend on spacing, size, and particle distribution.

contexts.context: Pages 5–7 discuss how cubic precipitates interact with migrating grain boundaries and how that interaction affects Zener-type pinning. The total system energy (particle plus boundary) depends on particle orientation; as a boundary moves it changes grain boundary area and the inclusion–matrix interface energy (coherent to incoherent), producing a rotation moment (force) on cubic particles. The paper gives a general relationship for that moment (for changes in the particle orientation angle) and notes the maximum moments are typically positive, so boundaries tend to rotate particles to increase the orientation angle. The magnitude of the moment depends on interaction angles, interfacial energy change, and coherency. Experimentally, the authors tested new Zener pinning equations using Ti-microalloyed and 321 austenitic steels with controlled isothermal and short anneals; particle size, volume fraction, and prior austenitic grain size were measured. Volume fractions were measured from thin-foil TEM techniques (known foil thickness, fringe/extinction methods) and extraction replicas, mean particle size from ~1000-particle statistics, and grain size by optical microscopy with ASTM linear intercept and planimetric analysis. These measurements were used to evaluate limiting grain size vs particle size/volume fraction in annealing.

The main objectives of the experimental work were to study in detail the interaction of particles with grain boundaries using transmission electron microscopy, and hence to check the validity of the new Zener pinning equations derived.

For each heat treatment both air cooled and water quenched samples were studied, the former for particle volume fraction measurements and the latter for particle size and prior austenitic grain size measurements.

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights factors relevant to Zener pinning during annealing. It states that for a given second-phase volume fraction, smaller precipitate sizes are more effective at pinning grain-boundary motion, and that elongated particle morphologies provide stronger retardation of grain growth. Coherent particle/matrix interfaces can increase pinning potency through interfacial and misfit-generated elastic energies. Precipitate size grows with isothermal hold time and more rapidly at higher annealing temperatures (notably above the Curie point for ferrite), while precipitation in austenite is relatively slow (C-curve with a nose around 900–930 °C) and can be substantially suppressed depending on coiling/annealing strategy. NbC particles typically show an aspect ratio ~2–2.5 and may change morphology (e.g., to more spherical and incoherent upon transformation), which also affects their pinning effectiveness. These observations collectively bear on how particle size, volume fraction, morphology, interface coherence, and processing thermal history control the limiting grain size via Zener-type pinning during annealing.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt is a bibliography from Graux et al. (2019) that collects key literature on Zener pinning, grain-boundary pinning by particles, and analytical/numerical modelling of grain growth in the presence of precipitates. It cites foundational theoretical treatments (e.g., an Acta Metallurgica overview on grain boundary pinning, "Overview no. 62"), historical and review material on the Zener equation ("Five decades of the Zener equation"), and focused studies on the relationship between pinning force and the limiting grain radius (Rios, Scripta Mater.). Also included are papers on grain boundary pinning by specific precipitates (Al6Mn) and on modelling precipitate nucleation/growth and their effect on grain growth (Andersen & Grong; Perez et al.). Collectively these references address how precipitate characteristics (size, number/volume fraction, growth/dissolution during heat treatment) determine the pinning force and therefore the limiting austenite grain size during annealing. The list points to both theoretical Zener-type relations and more advanced treatments that include precipitate evolution during non-isothermal annealing.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports thermomechanical homogenization conditions used for the cast nickel-based superalloy VDM780. STA analysis guided temperature selection, and a preheating/homogenization temperature of 1160 °C was chosen as suitable to eliminate the as-cast structure and as the inter-pass preheat. During rolling the workpiece was maintained roughly in the 1080–1120 °C range (accounting for a typical 50–60 °C drop when removed from the furnace). Heating to study heat-treatment behavior used a 10 °C/min ramp up to 1300 °C. The thermomechanical cycle involved five rolling passes on a two-high mill at a constant strain rate of 4 s⁻¹; after each pass the sample was returned to the furnace within ~10 s to encourage post-dynamic recrystallization, and samples were air-cooled after the final pass. The paper emphasizes elimination of interdendritic as-cast phases (Nb-enriched, γ/Laves-like) via homogenization. The excerpt does not provide explicit isothermal hold times for homogenization, nor general sub-solvus vs super-solvus temperature ranges or quantitative grain-growth rates during annealing, so direct broader recommendations beyond the VDM780 conditions above are not given.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports practical, alloy-level observations for powder-metallurgy Ni-based c–c¢ superalloys. The c¢ solvus is given as Tc¢–s. between 1100 C and 1160 C; solution heat treatment (SHT) above this solvus produces a uniform c-grain structure ~20–40 µm. Service requirements contrast bore (fine-grained, 5–10 µm) and rim (coarse-grained, 50–80 µm). c-grain evolution occurs rapidly during heating to the solution temperature and is likely governed by static recrystallization; the final grain-size distribution is set by the density of recrystallized nuclei whose boundaries consume residual work-hardened grains. Materials with limited residual work hardening can develop heterogeneous structures with abnormal grains >200 µm, which reduce fatigue resistance. Nanometric oxide ((Hf, Zr)O2) and carbide ((Nb, Ti)C) particles exert pinning pressure that stabilizes grain structure once work hardening is removed. A slower heating rate (notably the 2 C/min ramp cited) increases the likelihood of excessive grain growth on slightly work-hardened material — possibly due to ripening/dissolution of c¢ precipitates during ramp-up. Cooling rate from SHT controls subsequent c¢ precipitation (sub-solvus aging).
contexts.question: Practical temperature and time recommendations for homogenization of nickel superalloys, sub-solvus and super-solvus heat treatment, and grain growth during annealing at specific temperatures

contexts.context: The paper reports practical heat-treatment conditions and simulated effects on grain growth for a PM nickel-base superalloy. They set furnace temperature ~20–40 °C above the c0 solvus (1150 C used). Transient FEM simulations examined a 1150 C treatment with holding times of 15–120 min; local point temperatures converge with time. At 60 min, bore points A–C were <1110 C while rim points D–F were 1110–1145 C; inner regions (A–C) showed slow grain growth, outer regions (E–F) rapid growth. To obtain a dual microstructure the authors recommend holding time <60 min. Monte Carlo grain-growth simulation produced region-specific grain-size grades: A,B,C = 9–10; D = 7.5–8; E = 6–6.5; F = 5.5–6. They state that keeping the disk bore temperature below ~1110 C while maintaining rim temperatures ~1130–1150 C yields coarse-grained bore (grade ~9–10) and finer rim (~5.5–6). The study emphasizes temperature gradients and hold time control for sub-/super-solvus style dual microstructure and grain growth management.

contexts.context: This paper reports empirical relationships between grain size and solution temperature and between grain size and solution holding time for a powder-metallurgy (P/M) nickel-base superalloy. The authors also used a finite-element model to simulate regional microstructural differences and grain-size evolution during heat treatment. A practical outcome is a recommended condition to produce a dual microstructure in disks: keep the treating temperature of the disk bore below 1110 °C (reported as producing ~9–10 ASTM grain grade) while holding the disk rim at about 1130–1150 °C (reported as ~5.5–6 grade). The work therefore provides temperature targets for producing distinct fine/coarse grain regions via differential heating during solution treatment. The paper does not, in the provided excerpt, list specific homogenization times, explicit sub‑solvus versus super‑solvus labels, or quantitative grain-growth kinetics at particular annealing temperatures, but it indicates that grain-size vs temperature/time relationships were determined and can be modeled to guide heat-treatment design.

contexts.context: The excerpt gives practical heat-treatment conditions used for homogenization, solution (sub‑/super‑solvus) treatments and grain growth for Ni superalloys (Alloy 718, Allvac 718Plus and Waspaloy). Furnace heating was 0.3 °C/s with enforced argon cooling to 500 °C and cooling rates >0.3 °C/min. For homogenization of cast Allvac 718Plus the study used 1125 °C-5 h (below γ‑Laves eutectic), 1200 °C-5 h (above eutectic), and a combined 1125 °C-5 h + 1200 °C-5 h. For Alloy 718, short/standard solution treatments were 954 °C-1 h and 982 °C-1 h (giving different amounts of δ‑phase), while long exposure at 954 °C-15 h produces large amounts of δ. Large grain sizes were produced by treatments such as 1050 °C-3 h + 954 °C-1 h and 1020 °C-1 h. Allvac 718Plus used repeated short solution steps (954 °C-0.5 h, repeated 3×) for stress relief. For Waspaloy, solution heat treatments of 996–1080 °C for 4 h were used, with the higher temperatures producing larger grain size. These documented temperatures and dwell times provide practical starting points for homogenization, sub‑/super‑solvus processing and controlled grain growth during annealing.

contexts.context: The study used a solution heat treatment of 1338 K for 1200 s followed by an ice-water quench to fully dissolve precipitates before grain-growth testing. Isothermal grain-growth experiments were performed at 1353–1473 K with holding times from 180 to 2400 s. The initial average grain size after solution treatment was ≈70 µm. Grain size increases with both holding time and temperature: at 1353 K the grain sizes at 600, 1500 and 2400 s were 106, 154 and 192 µm respectively; at a 540 s hold, grain sizes at 1413, 1443 and 1473 K were 160, 238 and 299 µm respectively. Grain growth follows a parabolic-type law G^n_t − G^n_0 = k_G t with k_G = k0 exp(−Q/RT). The authors identified n = 2.43 and fitted k0 and Q such that G2.43_t − G2.43_0 = 3.63 × 10^14 exp[−3.2 × 10^5/(RT)] t. Twin boundaries were merged for average-size evaluation and size was computed by an area-fraction-weighted method.

contexts.context: This study reports experimental and modelling data on grain growth in a commercial Ni-based superalloy. A solution (homogenization) treatment used in the experiments was 1338 K for 1200 s to dissolve precipitates. Isothermal anneals for grain growth were performed between 1353 and 1473 K with holding times from 180 to 2400 s; the grain structure continuously coarsened as holding time and temperature increased. Grain growth followed a near-parabolic law (exponent slightly above 2), indicating boundary-migration-controlled kinetics with only minor pinning by precipitates. The authors found that grain-boundary mobility increases with temperature. A cellular automaton model was developed that reproduces the dependence of grain size on holding time and temperature. The paper also cites literature noting precipitate pinning (e.g., NbC and TiN) can retard growth and that boundary morphology at high temperature influences growth behavior. These experimental temperature/time ranges and kinetic observations provide concrete reference points for homogenization and annealing-driven grain growth in a Ni-based superalloy.
contexts.question: Practical temperature and time recommendations for homogenization of nickel superalloys, sub-solvus and super-solvus heat treatment, and grain growth during annealing at specific temperatures

snippets: The grain growth behavior in a typical Ni-based superalloy was investigated using isothermal heat treatment experiments over a holding temperature range of 1353–1473 K.
The experimental results showed that the grain structure continuously coarsened as the holding time and holding temperature increased during heat treatment.
A classical parabolic grain growth model was used to explore the mechanism of grain growth under experimental conditions.
The grain growth exponent was found to be slightly above 2.
This indicates that the current grain growth in the studied superalloy is mainly governed by grain boundary migration with a minor pinning effect from the precipitates.
The grain boundary mobility was found to increase with increasing temperature.
First, solution heat treatment was conducted for all specimens under a holding temperature of 1338 K for 1200 s.
Then, specimens were subjected to isothermal heating under a holding temperature of 1353–1473 K and holding time of 180–2400 s.
The developed CA model is capable of capturing the dependence of the grain size on the holding time under different holding temperatures.

contexts.context: This study on a powder-metallurgy nickel-base superalloy reports solution (heat) treatment in the range 950–1150 °C for 5–60 min with water quenching. A gradient/controlled solution treatment produced a dual microstructure (fine-grain bore grade ~9–10 and coarse-grain rim grade ~5.5–6). At 1010 °C grain growth with holding time is slow: grain size increased only ~4 μm when holding time increased from 5 to 45 min. Grain growth accelerates markedly between ~1110–1150 °C; at 1150 °C the grain size reached about 43 μm (grade ~5.5). Specimens were prepared from PREP powder (50–100 μm), HIP consolidated, then hot-deformed at 1090 °C (strain rate ≈5×10⁻² s⁻¹, strain =0.7) to produce a fine-grained starting microstructure before solution treatment. Grain sizes were measured by optical microscopy (image analysis). These experimental results give practical anchor points: slow growth near ~1010 °C over tens of minutes, rapid growth above ~1110 °C, and substantial coarsening by 1150 °C. The data are specific to the alloy chemistry and processing route used here (Table 1).

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a vertical internal-heating vacuum annealing furnace and its temperature control strategy. Typical working temperatures are indicated as approximately 600–1000°C (with a listed rated operating range of about 850–1400°C and a maximum of 1600°C). The furnace is specified to operate at high vacuum (Working vacuum 8 × 10–3 Pa) with a listed vacuum boost/degradation rate of 6.7 Pa/h. Temperature uniformity and control accuracy targets are tight (±5°C uniformity, ±3°C control accuracy). Heat transfer in the furnace is dominated by thermal radiation from the heater to the workpiece, with weak convective transfer and conductive paths to the furnace walls. Temperature feedback is provided by control and monitoring thermocouples in three zones and nine workpiece thermocouples for uniformity assessment. Closed-loop control is implemented via PLC; a GA-optimized fuzzy-PID tunes PID parameters and outputs are sent through a 4–20 mA signal to a power regulator that uses thyristor switching (variable period) to control heating power and zone temperatures. The excerpt does not provide detailed comparative advantages vs. atmosphere annealing or specific heating/cooling rate guidelines for steels.
contexts.question: What are the advantages and specific practices of vacuum furnace annealing compared to atmosphere furnace annealing? Vacuum level, heating rate, cooling rate guidelines for vacuum annealing of steels.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses vacuum carburizing (a vacuum-based thermochemical process) and highlights advantages relevant to vacuum furnace processing: reduced near-surface intergranular oxidation, reduced quenching distortion, cleaner/bright surfaces, the ability to run at much higher operating temperatures (reported up to ~1100°C) which shortens process times and increases productivity, and greater equipment reliability due to carbon–graphite heating chambers. Compressed gas quenching in vacuum processes lowers distortion and leaves a cleaner surface but requires higher hardenability to obtain full martensite. The text also emphasizes that higher carburizing temperatures can cause austenite grain coarsening and that microalloying (Mo + Nb) and specific pre-heat treatment cycles (an austenitizing/isothermal anneal cycle and a subcritical anneal cycle) are used to control austenite grain growth. The excerpt does not provide specific vacuum levels (mbar/torr), nor numerical heating-rate or cooling-rate guidelines for vacuum annealing of steels.

snippets: Vacuum carburizing with high-pressure gas quenching is increasingly employed to reduce near-surface intergranular oxidation and reduce quenching distortion.
Vacuum carburizing can be conducted at higher operating temperatures, as high as 1100  C, to reduce the processing times and increase furnace productivity.

This process also provides greater equipment reliability because  it uses heating chambers based on carbon–graphite  materials, which have good operating characteristics in a carbon-containing atmo- sphere  at elevated temperatures (Ref 4).
When compressed gas quenching is employed, higher hardenability is required to achieve a fully      martensitic microstructure.
Microalloying with grain growth inhibitors is one effective method to limit austenite grain growth of carburized steels (Ref 10).
The first pre-heat treatment cycle, labeled "pre-heat  treatment  cycle  1,'"  involved  austenitizing  and isothermal annealing stages (Fig. 1(a)).
The second pre-heat treatment cycle, labeled "pre-heat treatment cycle 2,'" consisted of a subcritical annealing stage (Fig. 1(b)).

Additions of Nb alone are found to provide some control of abnormal austenite grain growth. Additions of Mo in combination with Nb provide enhanced resistance to austenite grain growth, especially at high carburizing temperatures up to 1050  C.

contexts.context: The excerpt studies columnar grain growth in vacuum-degassed (semi-processed) non-oriented electrical steel after temper rolling, using a stepped-temperature annealing schedule in a laboratory furnace (Nabertherm). Annealing was performed in a pure hydrogen atmosphere and parameters were chosen to reflect industrial practice. The authors designed two heating rates (V1, V2) and two temperature/time steps (T1,t1; T2,t2) to preserve a primary recrystallized mid-thickness microstructure while nucleating and growing surface grains into a columnar structure via strain-induced grain boundary motion. A key experimental finding is that a heating rate above a critical value Vcr is required to initiate columnar grain growth; they obtained columnar microstructure at V1 ≈ 10 °C/s, whereas lower heating rates (e.g. V2) produced a polygonal microstructure. The paper reports use of EBSD for texture analysis and a sheet thickness of 0.6 mm. The excerpt does not provide numeric vacuum levels, explicit cooling rate guidelines, or a direct comparison of vacuum versus atmosphere furnace annealing advantages.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a GA-optimized fuzzy-PID temperature control system for a vacuum annealing furnace and highlights practical aspects and benefits of vacuum annealing. Advantages noted include energy savings, shorter production cycles, reduced labor intensity, improved product quality (notably for rare metals), reduced pollution and improved ecological environment through control of heating temperature and vacuum degree. Practical setup details: typical working temperatures are given as 600˚C–1000˚C with a maximum heating temperature up to 1200˚C; K-type thermocouples (measurement range 0˚C–1300˚C) are used for temperature sensing. Process sequence: load workpiece on a cart, close furnace door, evacuate the furnace, insert the workpiece into the heating chamber, then heat and cool according to a programmed temperature curve; PLC control is used (Siemens S7–1200, RS485/MODBUS) and heating begins only after the vacuum degree meets heating conditions. The paper focuses on optimizing control parameters to reduce temperature control error. The excerpt does not provide explicit numeric vacuum-level targets (e.g., torr or Pa) nor specific heating/cooling rate guidelines for steels.
contexts.question: What are the advantages and specific practices of vacuum furnace annealing compared to atmosphere furnace annealing? Vacuum level, heating rate, cooling rate guidelines for vacuum annealing of steels.

In the vacuum annealing process, reasonable and effective control of heating temperature and vacuum degree can be achieved to reduce system energy consumption, reduce pollution, and improve the ecological environment [21].
The heating environment of the vacuum annealing furnace is complex, with a maximum heating temperature of 1200˚C and a working temperature of 600˚C*1000˚C.

It is preferred to place the workpiece to be annealed on the cart, close the furnace door, vacuum the vacuum annealing furnace, and then push the workpiece into the heating chamber. Then, heat and cool according to the set temperature curve.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that vacuum-degassed non-oriented (NO) steel produced a columnar microstructure when processed using temper rolling plus an appropriate annealing schedule, exploiting the strain-induced boundary migration (SIBM) mechanism. Heating rate is critical: an adequate heating rate creates a temperature gradient through the sheet thickness that nucleates columnar grains at the surface while leaving mid-thickness fine grains unchanged. Laboratory schedules used two heating rates, V1 = 10 °C/s and V2 = 5 °C/s; the authors state that an insufficient heating rate (V2 < Vcr) leads to polygonal microstructure, while an appropriate heating rate (they indicate V1 ≈ 5 °C/s for this steel) promotes columnar grain growth. The authors list necessary practices as appropriate temper rolling, a sufficient heating rate, and application of ‘‘stepped-temperature’’ annealing with appropriate temperatures. The paper also shows experiments in a pure H2 atmosphere, but the columnar microstructure result is specifically reported for vacuum-degassed steel. The excerpt does not provide explicit vacuum pressure (vacuum level) values or detailed cooling-rate guidelines.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports laboratory melting of the VDM780 superalloy using a vacuum induction furnace (VIM) operated at about 10⁻³ mbar. The cast ingot composition was confirmed by spark optical emission spectroscopy. STA analysis (heating at 10 °C/min up to 1300 °C) guided selection of homogenization and preheating temperatures. A preheat/homogenization temperature of 1160 °C was chosen based on STA and to allow the sample to remain in the 1080–1120 °C range during rolling. Thermomechanical processing used an electric resistance furnace with laser-pyrometer temperature control; samples were rapidly (within ~10 s) returned to the furnace after deformation to promote post-dynamic recrystallization. The process avoided ESR and VAR remelting routes. During handling a typical exit temperature drop of ~50–60 °C was observed. The excerpt documents vacuum level, temperature control, transfer timing, and analytical checks (OES, STA), but does not provide detailed contamination-control measures (e.g., gas backfilling, getter use, crucible materials, residual gas analysis) or explicit procedures to limit pickup during heat treatment.

snippets: a vacuum induction furnace (10⁻³ mbar) was used.
The chemical composition of the alloy was verified using a spark optical emission spectrometer.
To determine suitable temperatures for heat treatment and homogenization, a STA SDT650 device was used with a heating rate of 10 °C/min up to 1300 °C.
The thermomechanical processing was carried out using an electric resistance furnace for both homogenizing (preheating) and reheating between deformation passes.
the sample was rapidly transferred (within 10 seconds) back to the furnace to facilitate post-dynamic recrystallization.

A temperature of 1160 °C was selected for two primary reasons: first, based on the STA analysis results it was found to be suitable for homogenization and elimination of the as-cast structure; second, it serves as an appropriate preheating temperature between rolling passes.
Therefore, by utilizing a preheating temperature of 1160 °C, the sample temperature during forming was maintained in the range of 1080–1120 °C.
During rolling, the sample typically experiences a temperature drop of approximately 50 to 60 °C upon exiting the furnace and being placed under the rolling mills.
without the use of electroslag remelting (ESR) or vacuum arc remelting (VAR).

contexts.context: This paper describes a vacuum annealing furnace (vertical internal heating) rather than an induction melting furnace, but provides useful quantitative and control-system details relevant to vacuum heat treatment. The reported working vacuum is 8 × 10–3 Pa. Typical annealing temperatures are given as about 600–1000°C (with process table entries up to 1600°C and a rated operating range 850–1400°C), target uniformity ±5°C and control accuracy ±3°C. Heat transfer in the furnace is dominated by radiation from heaters to workpieces, with some conductive and weak convective components. Heat-loss terms include conduction to furnace walls, thermal short-circuiting, and losses via instrumentation/vacuum penetrations: “heat exported from thermocouples and vacuum tubes, as well as the dissipation of heat from the observation holes.” Temperature measurement and control use multiple thermocouples (control/monitoring in three zones and nine around the workpiece area), PLC communication over RS485 to upper computer software, and a GA-optimized PID (GA-Fuzzy-PID) that outputs via PLC and a 4–20 mA link to a power regulator controlling thyristors. The paper focuses on temperature control; it notes potential leakage/heat-loss paths through penetrations that are also relevant to vacuum integrity and contamination control, but it does not provide detailed contamination-control procedures specific to induction melting/remelting.

contexts.context: This excerpt addresses temperature-control challenges in vacuum annealing furnaces and a proposed GA-optimized fuzzy-PID solution. It states traditional PID control yields high temperature fluctuation, large overshoot, and long response times. Vacuum annealing systems are described as time-varying, nonlinear, and having time delays, with strong coupling between different zone temperatures and between annealing temperature and vacuum degree, so temperature precision directly affects product quality. Fuzzy-PID has been used to improve control without requiring an accurate model, but its rule tables are subjective and may not be optimal. The authors report combining equipment parameters and using a genetic algorithm to optimize the fuzzy-PID controller, with simulation and offline experiments showing improved temperature accuracy, rise time, and overshoot versus fuzzy-PID and traditional PID. The excerpt does not provide specifics on vacuum-induction melting/remelting, target vacuum levels, contamination sources or mitigation (e.g., residual gases, gettering, vacuum bakeouts), pumping systems, or metallurgical issues during melting and remelting of steels and superalloys.
contexts.question: Vacuum induction furnace for melting and remelting steels and superalloys, vacuum level and contamination control during heat treatment

contexts.context: This study concerns vacuum-carburizing steels and compares pseudo-carburizing and pre-heat treatment cycles. Analytical methods included TEM/STEM at 200 keV with EDS and TEM foil preparation by twin-jet electro-polishing in 5% HClO4 + 95% CH3COOH. The authors observed that conventional box-furnace heat treatments produced oxidation and decarburization surface layers up to about 200 lm thick, so microstructural analysis was carried out on central regions to avoid surface-affected material. Pseudo-carburizing cycles used higher temperatures and longer holds than the listed pre-heat cycles (examples: 900 C for 1 h then held at 650 C for 1.5 h; and 700 C for 3 h), and microalloying additions had a stronger influence on microstructure after the higher-temperature pseudo-carburizing treatments. The paper thus documents surface contamination/decaburization risks associated with non-vacuum box-furnace processing and provides experimental parameters and metallographic practices relevant to avoiding or accounting for surface-altered regions.

contexts.context: The excerpt does not discuss vacuum induction furnaces or specify vacuum levels, but it provides practical guidance on atmosphere and contamination control during heat treatment. It emphasizes protecting steel or cast iron from scaling and decarburization and notes that commercial furnaces are generally equipped for atmosphere control (for example, by burning controlled gas/air in a muffle) and that water vapor from combustion is detrimental and should be removed. Where built-in control is absent, external atmosphere generators can be used and their gas piped to multiple furnaces. Short-term protective measures include covering parts with cast-iron borings or chips. Salt and lead baths are mentioned as alternatives that simplify prevention of scaling/decarburization because the work is surrounded by liquid. Accurate temperature measurement and control are important; thermocouple life and selection depend on maximum temperature and furnace atmosphere, and thermocouples should be protected and placed close to the work. Furnace design should prevent direct flame impingement on parts. Overall, the excerpt supplies atmosphere-control and contamination-reduction strategies useful for heat treatment, but it lacks vacuum-specific parameters and procedures.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports vacuum annealing/tempering of AISI 431 stainless steel using a vacuum tempering/annealing process: low-temperature vacuum anneals of 2, 4 and 6 hours were applied (VA1–VA3) and a 150 mm × 10 mm disk was ‘‘hardened to its saturated maximum by a vacuum tempering operation’’. The treatment is claimed to increase surface hardness (up to ~150%) and to form hardened surface layers; wear was evaluated on pin-on-disk apparatus. Material composition (AISI 431) is provided and standard metallographic preparation and etching (emery papers, diamond paste polishing, oxalic acid etch) and microstructure capture (Clemex analyser) are described. The excerpt, however, does not give numerical vacuum levels (pressures), gas species, contamination sources, gettering, backfilling practice, furnace design, crucible/inoculation materials, or specific contamination-control procedures that are required when melting/remelting steels and superalloys in vacuum induction furnaces. Thus it provides context that vacuum heat treatment was used and its effects on AISI 431, but no operational vacuum-level or contamination-control specifics for vacuum induction melting/remelting.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a vacuum annealing furnace temperature-control system that emphasizes coordinating vacuum level and temperature to improve process outcomes and reduce contamination, energy use, and pollution. The authors propose a GA-optimized fuzzy-PID controller to tune kp, ki and kd for better temperature control. Process steps require placing the workpiece, closing the furnace, evacuating the chamber, and initiating heating only when the vacuum degree meets heating conditions. Temperature sensing uses K-type thermocouples (measurement range 0˚C–1300˚C) and typical annealing working temperatures are cited as 600–1000˚C (with furnace capability to 1200˚C). Hardware architecture centers on a Siemens S7–1200 PLC with DI/DO/AI/AO modules; setpoint temperature curves are sent from an upper computer to the PLC over RS485 using MODBUS. This information touches on vacuum initiation criteria, sensing and control hardware, and an optimization approach for PID tuning relevant to controlling contamination and thermal conditions during vacuum heat treatments.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses vacuum carburizing (a vacuum-based heat-treat process) and highlights its advantages for contamination control and high‑temperature processing. Vacuum carburizing with high‑pressure gas quenching reduces near‑surface intergranular oxidation (IGO) and quenching distortion and produces cleaner surfaces compared with conventional gas carburizing. The process can be run at elevated temperatures (up to ~1100 °C), shortening cycle times and increasing productivity, but higher temperatures risk austenite grain coarsening that can degrade fatigue performance. Vacuum systems here use carbon–graphite heating chambers that perform well in carbon-containing atmospheres at high temperatures. Compressed‑gas quenching in vacuum furnaces yields cleaner parts versus oil quenching, though it demands higher hardenability to obtain full martensite. For control of microstructure during high‑temperature vacuum heat treatments, microalloying (Nb and Mo) and formation of small carbo‑nitride precipitates can inhibit austenite grain growth; Mo+Nb combinations are particularly effective up to ~1050 °C through solute and precipitation effects. The excerpt does not provide specific vacuum pressure (mbar/Torr) values, vacuum‑induction melting details, or explicit contaminant partial‑pressure limits for melting/remelting.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports vacuum annealing experiments on AISI 431 martensitic stainless steel and describes specimen preparation and characterization methods. Samples were etched with oxalic acid, rinsed and ‘‘vapour degreased’’ before imaging; microstructures were captured with a Clemex picture analyser. Wear testing (pin-on-disk) produced weight-loss values for untreated and three vacuum-annealed conditions (UNT 0.0066 g; VA1 0.0039 g; VA2 0.0027 g; VA3 0.005 g). Case depths decreased with annealing (untreated ~19 µm; annealed samples ~16, 12, 8.5 µm). Annealing produced grain coarsening of martensite, increasing ductility and reducing hardness. The authors note possible surface nitride (iron nitride) formation associated with discontinuous white areas on the hardened surface. The paper documents vacuum annealing as a heat-treatment method and highlights surface preparation and microstructural/tribological outcomes but does not provide details on vacuum furnace type (e.g., vacuum induction melting), target vacuum levels/pressures, bakeouts, gettering, partial pressures, or active contamination-control measures during melting/remelting of steels or superalloys.
